title: Bulgari Diagono Scuba GMT
description: There are plenty of watches that blur the line between sport and luxury, but few do so with the kind of conviction found in the Bulgari Diagono Scuba GMT . Born from Bulgari ’s distinctly Roman design language, the Diagono collection has always done things a bit differently. Where its contemporaries leaned heavily into convention, the Diagono embraced bold typography, integrated construction, and a kind of architectural presence that feels as much designed as it does engineered. The Scuba GMT takes that foundation and adds a layer of real-world utility. At approximately 40mm in stainless steel, the case strikes a versatile balance — substantial enough to feel like a proper tool watch, yet refined enough to wear comfortably day to day. The engraved bezel, signed with Bulgari’s unmistakable double-logo, frames a matte black dial fitted with luminous indices, bold handset, and an additional GMT hand, allowing for the tracking of a second time zone with ease. It’s a practical complication, executed here without unnecessary flourish. This is a watch that reveals its purpose in the details. Screw-down pushers at 2 and 4 o’clock allow for adjustment of the second time zone, while a corrector at 8 o’clock handles the date — an idiosyncratic but highly functional layout that underscores the Diagono’s tool-watch credentials. A sapphire crystal and 200 meters of water resistance round out the package, ensuring it’s as capable as it is distinctive. Powering the watch is an automatic, COSC-certified movement, offering reliable timekeeping and a robust platform for its dual-time functionality. It’s not about decoration here — it’s about dependability, in keeping with the watch’s broader ethos. What truly sets this example apart, however, is its bracelet. Fitted with a matching integrated stainless steel multi-link bracelet, it transforms the wearing experience entirely, giving the watch a cohesive, almost seamless profile on the wrist. This bracelet — designed to flow directly from the case — lends the Diagono a distinctly elevated presence when compared to its rubber-strapped counterparts. Offered with its inner box, this example captures the essence of what makes the Diagono Scuba GMT so compelling: a watch that refuses to conform, yet succeeds precisely because of it. Equal parts sport watch and design object, it’s a reminder that utility doesn’t have to come at the expense of personality.

title: Jaeger-LeCoultre Reverso Classic Medium
description: The Reverso is, in essence, one of the very first sports wristwatches. Despite its refined and elegant appearance, it combined utility with a sleek Art Deco exterior that has become an icon of horological design. But in the case of the Reverso, looks deceive. Though the model appears at first glance a dress watch, it owes its existence to the gentlemanly sport of polo. While touring India in the early 1930s, Swiss businessman César de Trey met a British polo player who'd just cracked the crystal of his watch on the field. De Trey returned to Switzerland with an idea already taken root in his head, which he then discussed with Jacques-David LeCoultre: What if a watch could be made with a case that could be completely flipped over? The reversible case, masterminded by designer Alfred Chauvot, was Jaeger-LeCoultre's solution to the problems polo players faced on the field. Once reversed, the delicate components of the case were protected from the shocks sustained while on horseback. No more would polo players crack the crystals or damage the dials of their watches. Chauvot took out a patent for the Reverso in March 1931. Produced chiefly in steel, Jaeger-LeCoultre also released the Reverso in gold, and decorated the caseback with engravings or even miniature portraits. This particular Reverso Classic features a 25.5mm stainless steel reversible case featuring an unadorned caseback on the reverse side. The main silvered timekeeping dial features a classic 'Arabic'-numeral layout with subsidiary seconds, a blued steel 'sword' handset, and a guilloche dial center. Powered by an in-house manually wound movement, it comes fitted to a brown leather strap with a signed stainless steel pin buckle. Simple and refined, this is the type of watch one could conceivably wear for a lifetime and never go out of style — and that's a difficult thing to turn down.

title: Croton Chronomaster Aviator Sea Diver
description: In an era defined by exploration — of oceans, skies, and the most remote corners of the globe — watchmakers were tasked with building instruments that could keep up. Few brands embodied that spirit quite like Nivada Grenchen . By the time Admiral Richard E. Byrd returned from Operation Deep Freeze with glowing praise for the brand’s durability, its reputation had already been forged in some of the harshest conditions on Earth. What followed was a run of purpose-built tool watches that remain compelling to this day. Chief among them is the Chronomaster Aviator Sea Diver — a watch that, as its name suggests, was designed to do just about everything. Introduced in the early 1960s, it combined the functionality of a chronograph with the added utility of a rotating dive bezel, answering the growing demand for multi-purpose sports watches at a time when specialization was still taking shape. It’s a format that feels familiar now, but at the time, it was quietly forward-thinking. This particular example, dating to the 1970s and dialed 'Croton' for the U.S. market, showcases the model in one of its more refined later executions. Gone are the earlier 'broad arrow' hands, replaced here by slimmer stick hands that lend the dial a more restrained, legible quality. The overall aesthetic remains unmistakably vintage: a densely informative dial balanced by crisp sub-registers, paired with a bidirectional timing/12-hour bezel that adds both visual contrast and real utility. Sharp, faceted lugs anchor the case, giving it a presence on the wrist that belies its modest proportions. What sets this example apart, however, is its bracelet. Fitted with a matching steel multi-link bracelet featuring a signed fold-over clasp, it offers a level of completeness — and wearability — that’s increasingly difficult to find. Period-correct bracelets for these models are rare, and they transform the watch into something that feels fully realized rather than merely preserved. Powering the watch is a manually wound, Swiss-made chronograph movement — robust, proven, and entirely in keeping with the Chronomaster’s tool-watch ethos. It’s a movement built not for show, but for reliability, echoing the very qualities that made these watches indispensable to explorers decades ago. At approximately 38mm in stainless steel, the case strikes a sweet spot: large enough to feel contemporary, yet compact enough to retain its vintage charm. On the wrist, it wears with an easy versatility that mirrors its original intent — a watch equally at home in the air, at sea, or simply navigating the rhythms of everyday life. In all, the Chronomaster Aviator Sea Diver stands as a reminder of a time when watches were built to go anywhere — and expected to make it back. This example, complete with its correct bracelet, captures that spirit in full.

title: Tudor Pelagos Blue
description: Modern dive watches tend to fall into one of two camps: those that trade on nostalgia, and those that genuinely push the category forward. The Tudor Pelagos M25600TB-0001 is firmly in the latter. While its design nods to the brand’s historic tool watches, the Pelagos was conceived from the ground up as a contemporary instrument — one that prioritizes function, durability, and thoughtful engineering over sentimentality. Executed entirely in titanium, the 42mm case is both lightweight and exceptionally robust, outfitted with a helium escape valve and rated to a formidable 500 meters of water resistance. The matte blue dial — matched to a unidirectional ceramic bezel with a luminous insert — is purpose-built for legibility, with bold square markers, an outer minute track, and the brand’s signature ‘snowflake’ handset. Everything here is designed to be read quickly, clearly, and without distraction, whether at depth or on dry land. It’s the bracelet, however, that quietly sets the Pelagos apart. Fitted with Tudor’s spring-loaded, self-adjusting clasp, it automatically compensates for changes in wrist size — whether from temperature shifts or a wetsuit compressing at depth. It’s an elegant solution to a real-world problem, and one that underscores the watch’s no-nonsense approach to utility. Inside beats the in-house, COSC-certified Calibre MT5612 automatic movement, offering a 70-hour power reserve and a silicon balance spring for enhanced resistance to magnetism and shocks. It’s a modern workhorse in every sense — precise, durable, and built for daily wear. This example is offered as a complete set, with its full kit and papers dated February 2023, adding a level of collectibility and assurance that complements the watch’s already compelling proposition. Taken as a whole, the Pelagos isn’t trying to be everything to everyone — it’s simply one of the most intelligently designed dive watches of the modern era. A true tool watch, executed without compromise.

title: TAG Heuer Monaco Chronograph 'Racing Green' Limited Edition
description: Some watches are iconic within the world of horology. Others break through into something larger. The TAG Heuer Monaco has long occupied that rarified space — an instantly recognizable design whose cultural footprint extends well beyond the wrist. From its debut in 1969 as one of the first automatic chronographs to its enduring association with Steve McQueen in Le Mans , the Monaco has never really left the spotlight. This modern interpretation, Reference CAW218E.FC6565, leans into that legacy while offering a more contemporary take on the formula. The 39mm square case — executed here in lightweight, bead-blasted titanium — retains the Monaco’s signature geometry, complete with its left-side crown, a nod to the original Calibre 11 models. It’s a shape that remains as unconventional today as it was over half a century ago, and all the more compelling for it. The dial is where things take a subtle turn: Rendered in a simple silver tone with a sunray finish, it features bold green 'squircle'-shaped chronograph totalizers, mint-colored luminous 'dot' indices and luminous 'sword' hands, and a restrained palette that feels both modern and faithful to the Monaco’s design language. The outer applied indices are subtle yet funky, while the overall layout remains clean, balanced, and unmistakably Monaco. Powering the watch is the Calibre 11 automatic chronograph movement, visible through a sapphire caseback. While its name recalls the groundbreaking movement of 1969, today’s iteration offers modern reliability and performance, pairing vintage inspiration with contemporary execution. This particular example is offered as a full set, complete with its inner and outer boxes, papers, leather travel pouch, and warranty card. It comes fitted to its correct green leather rally strap, secured by a signed titanium butterfly clasp — an ideal complement to the case and a detail that underscores the watch’s cohesive design. Bold, unconventional, and deeply rooted in motorsport history, the Monaco remains one of the few watches that can be identified from across a room. More than five decades on, it still feels like a statement.

title: A. Lange & Söhne Lange 1815 Tourbillon
description: There are tourbillons that announce themselves — and then there are those, like this A. Lange & Söhne 1815 Tourbillon ref. 730.079F , that quietly assert their authority. Introduced as part of Lange’s most classically minded family, the 1815 Tourbillon is already a study in restraint, but this enamel-dial variant elevates that ethos to something approaching purity. Limited to just 100 pieces and cased in platinum, it pairs one of the brand’s most technically ambitious calibers with one of its most traditional dial-making techniques. What sets the 1815 Tourbillon apart isn’t just the presence of a one-minute tourbillon at six o’clock, but what surrounds it — or rather, what doesn’t. The dial is rendered in fired white enamel over copper, a painstaking, multi-stage process that yields a glossy, flawless surface that can’t be rushed or corrected. Large Arabic numerals, a chemin-de-fer minutes track, and blued steel hands complete the picture, punctuated by a single red “12” — a subtle nod to historic Saxon pocket watches. The result is a dial that feels at once archival and immediate, traditional yet unmistakably Lange. The model distinguishes itself not only through its rarity, but through the clarity of its execution. The tourbillon aperture is framed by a black-polished bridge, drawing the eye without disrupting the symmetry of the design. Despite the presence of a highly complicated regulating organ, the watch remains remarkably discreet — an object that reveals its depth slowly, over time. Housed in a platinum case measuring 39.5mm in diameter and just over 11mm thick, it boasts the kind of balanced proportions that define the 1815 line, offering presence without excess. A white enamel dial with blued steel hands, Arabic numerals, and a red “12,” along with a one-minute tourbillon at 6 o’clock, are enough to signal its incredible bona fides. Flipping the watch over, the manually wound caliber L102.1 is on full display beneath a sapphire caseback. Here, Lange’s signature three-quarter plate architecture, hand-engraved balance cock, screwed gold chatons, and even a diamond endstone for the tourbillon axis underscore the brand’s uncompromising approach to finishing. More technically, the movement integrates both a stop-seconds mechanism for the tourbillon and a zero-reset function, allowing the seconds hand to snap precisely to zero when setting the time — an innovation that brings genuine chronometric utility to what is often a purely theoretical complication. This particular example is accompanied by its inner and outer boxes as well as its original papers and pamphlets. Paired to a signed black alligator leather strap with a signed platinum deployant clasp, it's a study in Teutonic restraint and quality that signals good taste from across the room. Whether the centerpiece of a larger collection or as the single occupant of an opulent watch box, there's no denying the 1815 Tourbillon's place in the pantheon of great modern timepieces.

title: Citizen Campanola 'Kasanekyo' 20th Anniversary
description: There’s a certain kind of watch that exists outside the usual categories — not quite Swiss, not entirely Japanese, and far more interesting because of it. Campanola, a high-end division of Citizen, was created as an exploration of what happens when traditional Japanese artistry meets the language of haute horlogerie. The name itself nods curiously to Italy, while the execution remains deeply rooted in Japan — particularly in its reverence for dial work. Over time, Campanola has become something of a connoisseur’s secret, quietly producing watches that feel more like objects of craft than conventional timepieces. This “Kasanekyo” 20th Anniversary model, Reference NZ0000-07L, embodies that philosophy. Released in 2020 to mark two decades of the line, it features a remarkable deep-blue urushi lacquer dial, hand-finished by master craftsman Tetsuo Gido. Layers of lacquer and subtle mother-of-pearl inlay create a sense of depth that shifts with the light — less a dial, more a miniature landscape. A big date display at 12 o’clock and a power reserve indicator at 6 add visual balance, while faceted, lancet-shaped hands — including a skeletonized minute hand — play across the surface with striking clarity. Inside, things get equally interesting. The automatic Caliber Y513, based on a La Joux-Perret movement, brings Swiss mechanical credibility to a distinctly Japanese aesthetic vision. Housed in a 42mm stainless steel case with Duratect hardening and a dual-curved sapphire crystal, it wears with presence but never feels heavy-handed. Unexpected, beautifully executed, and quietly obsessive in its attention to detail, this watch is IDEAL for someone looking for something just a bit different with a unique and fascinating backstory.

title: Omega 'Secret' Bracelet Watch by Gilbert Albert
description: There are jewelry watches. And then there are small, subversive sculptures that happen to tell time. This 1960s Omega watch belongs firmly in the latter category. Its elaborate 18K yellow-gold bracelet explodes across the wrist in a nugget-meets-lava formation. An organic, 'coral-like' lattice that feels less fabricated than unearthed. Created during one of Omega’s most adventurous design periods, the piece throws polite Swiss restraint out the window. The result is a watch that behaves more like a sculpture than timekeeper, a small eruption of gold across the wrist where form, texture, and imagination take the lead. The petite champagne dial—complete with slender 'baton' indices and 'sword' hands—has a truly elegant presence in a sea of gold. It’s intimacy by design. The crown is tucked discreetly on the caseback, preserving the uninterrupted sculptural silhouette. Inside beats a manual-wind Omega Calibre , a slim, reliable movement suited perfectly to refined ladies’ watches of the era. Omega has long understood that women’s watches can be mechanically serious and aesthetically daring. This piece proves it. Part avant-garde jewel, part timekeeping instrument, fully wearable art. A wildly confident design period in Omega’s history. One that deserves far more attention than it gets.

title: Breguet Tradition
description: Breguet’s timepieces are more than simple wristwatches — they’re works of art. Take the Tradition series, for example: Introduced in 2005, this decidedly avant-garde looking design actually echoes the movement architecture and open worked design of the firm’s 19th-century souscription pocketwatches. (Sourscription watches, for the non-French speakers in the house, were timepieces that were sold on a subscription basis — the buyer would put down 25%, with the rest due upon delivery.) This example, a Reference 7027, is a drop-dead gorgeous melding of classic Breguet aesthetics that grabs your attention and doesn't let go. Housed in a perfectly sized 37mm yellow gold case with Breguet's signature ribbed 'coin edge' mid-case, a sapphire crystal, and signed crown, it features a guilloché dial with ‘Breguet’ hands and ‘Roman’ numeral indices set atop a completely open-worked movement with gold-toned sandblasted 'frosted' main plate. This movement, the manually-wound Breguet Calibre 507 DR, is more than just the engine powering the watch’s time-keeping functions. With its straight-line lever escapement arranged along the bottom edge and 50-hour power reserve indicator, it bridges the gap between Breguet’s horological innovations of the 18th and 19th centuries with inventive, modern industrial design. Paired to a signed, black alligator leather strap with a signed yellow gold deployant clasp, this incredible piece is the perfect companion for in search of a cutting-edge expression of classic timekeeping.

title: Gucci Tiger's Eye Sautoir Timepiece Necklace
description: There’s something irresistibly glamorous about a not-so-secret watch that isn't worn on the wrist. In the 1960s and ’70s, houses like Gucci began to blur the line between horology and jewelry, creating pieces that were as much about presence as they were about precision. The sautoir — a long, draped, and unmistakably elegant necklace with a pendant — became a canvas for that idea. Less about checking the time, more about making an entrance. This Gucci tiger’s eye watch necklace is a perfect embodiment of that moment. Crafted in 18K yellow gold, the long, fluid chain catches the light with every movement, leading to a sculptural pendant measuring just over 4 inches. At its center sits a warm, chatoyant tiger’s eye dial — its natural striations shimmering between golden honey and deep brown depending on the angle. Matching tiger’s eye beads at the ends add a tactile, almost talismanic quality to the piece. Signed Gucci Italy and powered by a manually movement, it’s as thoughtfully made as it is visually striking. But make no mistake, this isn’t about precision timing without numerals or indices. It’s about mood. A louche jet-setting exceptionally Gucci mood! A watch, yes. But more than that, especially with Gucci mining this particular period on the fashion side, it's an ultra-stylish piece of jewelry with a real sense of timing.

title: Universal Genève White Shadow
description: You’re looking at one of Gérald Genta’s first notable watch designs. That’s right — before he put pen to paper and created the Royal Oak for Audemars Piguet and the Nautilus for Patek Philippe, the young Swiss designer made the White Shadow for Universal Genève. Elegant and comfortable, it’s one of the more interesting vintage dress watch designs both from an aesthetic and a technical perspective. Thanks to its record-breaking, slim automatic movement, the White Shadow is a supremely comfortable watch. The cushion shaped case is impressively thin, but it’s also wide enough to feel substantial. UG produced this model in several configurations, but this particular example has to be one of the handsomest we’ve seen. Dating to the 1970s, it features a 35mm stainless steel case, a signed crown, an acrylic crystal, a matching stainless steel bracelet with a signed UG clasp, and a grey sunburst dial with applied white baton indices and a matching white stick handset. What’s more, it’s powered by the Universal Genève Cal. 2-66 automatic movement with micro-rotor, and even comes with its case back sticker. There are lots of great affordable dress watches out there, but few can match the White Shadow’s pedigree. It’s elegant, innovative and unusual — quite the combination!

title: Bulova Accutron 'Breguet' Stainless Steel Dress Watch
description: In 1960, Bulova had a vision of the future. And in that future, there was humming. The Accutron was the world's first electronic watch. About a decade before the infamous Quartz Crisis, Bulova put into production a timepiece that did away with the traditional balance wheel, favoring instead a tuning fork design — a 360-hertz steel tuning fork powered by electromagnets attached to a battery-powered transistor oscillator circuit — as its timekeeper. Designed by Max Hetzel, the Accutron made waves, becoming the first wristwatch precise enough to be qualified for U.S. Railroad certification, guaranteed to be accurate to about one minute per month, or roughly two seconds per day. While Bulova had grand plans for their watch of the future, it never anticipated the success that the uniquely appointed Spaceview — a transparent Accutron with a view into the unique movement beneath — would generate. From this watch, the Accutron grew into a large collection. And while its cool technology was soon eclipsed by that of the quartz movement, it nevertheless represents a unique period in horological history, with collectors snapping up examples of vintage Accutrons whenever and wherever they can get their hands on them. This particular Accutron, dating to the 1970s, mixes its futuristic tech with vintage-inspired cues: Measuring 37mm in a brushed stainless steel 'C'-shaped case, it features a 4:30 crown, a polished bezel, an acrylic crystal, a screw-down caseback, and a silver dial with applied 'Breguet' indices, an outer 'dot' minute track, and a matching 'baton' handset. Powered by an electronic Accutron movement and paired to a blue saffiano leather strap, this nifty timekeeper is perfect for the collector who appreciates both futurism as well as horological history. Subtle and cool, it would make an excellent, casual addition to any watch roll. Accutron Factory Certified Pre-Owned Analog:Shift is proud to be partnered with Accutron as the exclusive retailer for Factory Certified Pre-Owned timepieces. Our longstanding relationship and shared passion for horological history and design have culminated in this unique offering. Each Accutron timepiece undergoes rigorous inspection and certification by expert watchmakers and is backed by the manufacture, ensuring unparalleled authenticity and quality.

title: Gevril Tribeca 'Paul Newman' Chronograph
description: The ‘Paul Newman’ Daytona — in all its various guises — is one of the most desirable watches in the world. Unfortunately, due to both scarcity and price, not every collector can acquire one. That’s where the Tribeca comes in. Manufactured by Gevril, a Swiss-founded watchmaker currently based in New York City with a history dating back to the mid-18th century, the Tribeca is a love letter to the ‘Paul Newman’ Daytona. Originally produced in 1990 in strictly limited series, the Tribeca models included homages to both steel as well as gold PNDs, making an aesthetic once available exclusively to the most well-heeled (or luckiest) collectors suddenly available to the masses. This particular Tribeca, a Reference R005/1, is housed in a 37mm stainless steel case with holey lugs, screw-down pushers, an acrylic crystal, and a stainless steel bezel with a black tachymeter scale insert. Featuring a white Tritium ‘exotic’ Paul Newman-style dial with black sub-registers, a matching baton handset, and an outer, 1/5th-second track, it’s individually numbered in red text in the 6:00 position. Powered by the workhorse ETA 2824 movement with an added Dubois Dépraz chronograph module, it comes paired to a stainless steel, solid-link 'Oyster-style' bracelet with a signed blade clasp. In fantastic condition, it even includes its goodies. Inner, outer boxes, and warranty card. If you’re in love with the idea of a ‘Paul Newman’ Daytona but aren’t in the position to, ahem, take out a second mortgage on your home to purchase one — then you owe it yourself to check out this Tribeca. Snag it today!

title: Tissot Telemeter 1938
description: Oftentimes in the hobby of watch collecting, there can be a bit too much focus on what the hot watch of the moment might be - the piece that no one can get, or the watch that everyone wants to own purely so they can say they own it. But in this frenzy of emphasis on what the next big windfall investment piece might be, there are literally tons of watches and brands which slip woefully under the radar. Tissot is among those brands. This particular Tissot is a modern beauty based on many of Tissot's most striking vintage designs back in the days of their incredible chronographs. This piece looks just like its vintage counterparts, but at a contemporary 42mm in diameter with a modern upgraded Valjoux movement. Outbound, this piece has a telemeter scale, a true rarity on watches made these days. If you love vintage designs but want something with the solidity and piece of mind of a modern watch, this is an excellent foray into the world of fine watchmaking. A brilliant look back into the historical archive, Tissot is every bit as relevant in the 2020s as in 1938, the year of this watch's namesake.

title: Patek Philippe Annual Calendar Flyback Chronograph
description: In 1996, Patek Philippe made one of its most brilliant strategic moves— the introduction and development of the Annual Calendar complication. Patek recognized a gap in offerings between the simple and (relatively) accessible Calatrava classic dress watches and the complicated, and in turn, highly expensive, Grand Complications. The so-called "Annual Calendar" bridged the gap between these two price points, offering a natural progression for collectors looking to access a Patek complication at a more accessible price point. The Annual Calendar needs adjusting just once per year. This change enables the movement to be far more economical to produce, easier to service, and more affordable to purchase. The inaugural Reference 5035 Annual Calendar of 1996 was a tremendous success, and ever since, Patek's Annual Calendars have been a popular favorite. Annual Calendars from Patek represent the bread and butter of the brand— perfectly executed, complicated dress watches. In 2006, Patek Philippe introduced the Reference 5960P, a platinum annual calendar featuring a flyback chronograph. Distinguished from the other annual calendars because of its decidedly sporty look, the 5960 stood out from the crowd. It wasn't only noteworthy for the flyback; however, it also featured a modern annual calendar layout. Instead of displaying the annual calendar in subsidiary dials, Patek instead used aperture displays. While apertures have been used by Patek throughout history (as seen in the Reference 3448 perpetual calendar, for example), they are usually displayed in-line. For the 5960, Patek instead made vaulted, framed apertures that follow the curve of the case. This gave the annual calendar a truly modern look and breathed new life into the collection. It's not for everyone, but it's an entirely new look. This particular Reference 5960P-001 is crafted in platinum, featuring an anthracite dial and red accents. The calendar complications and power reserve are positioned at the top of the dial, while the minute and hour totalizers are located at 6 o'clock within a single oversized sub-register. A day/night indicator is easy to miss within the subdial. The case is topped with a sapphire crystal. Several other stylistic choices were made with the 5960 that distinguished it from previous references. Unlike the 5035 and 5146 annual calendars, the 5960 features a concave bezel that helps reinforce its modern look. Additionally, the 5960 was sized at a comfortable 40.5mm—a sporty size for a Patek complication, but one that suits the rest of the watch's look. The 5960 is finished with a signed crown and engraved pump pushers. The aesthetics of this piece are classic and modern all at the same time. The 5960 is one of the most beloved modern Patek Philippe references and a great starting point for those interested in complicated watches, particularly sports watch enthusiasts. Rip off the bandaid with the 5960 and then go full send on a 3940. You'll thank us later.

title: Patek Philippe Calatrava
description: Patek Philippe has long been known for its complicated watchmaking, but following the stock market crash of 1929, fewer clients had the means to purchase these high-end timepieces. After securing a controlling interest in the firm, Jean and Charles Henri Stern decided to create a simple watch that would come in at a lower price point and appeal to the firm’s clientele. Using the principles espoused by the Bauhaus school and its minimalist, function-first philosophy, Patek conceived the Calatrava, taking its name from the Spanish military order established in the 12th century which had become the symbol of Patek Philippe. Since its debut in 1932, the Calatrava has become a byword for a simple, time-only dress watch — even from brands other than Patek itself. This piece, a Reference 6119G-001, builds on the foundational designs of its three-hand predecessors and is housed in a 39mm white gold case with a guilloché Clous de Paris bezel, signed crown, a sapphire crystal and a svelte case profile that will delight any wrist. The dial features a dynamic, vertically satin brushed anthracite tone acting as a canvas for its applied gold indices, the constant-seconds subsidiary register at 6 o’clock, a 'dauphine' handset and a white-accented ‘railroad’ minute track Powered and visible by way of a sapphire exhibition caseback, this Calatrava features a hand-wound Patek Philippe Calibre 30-255 PS and comes fitted to a signed black alligator leather strap with a signed 18K white gold pin buckle. A proper Calatrava from the world’s greatest watchmaker is forever.

title: Tudor Black Bay Fifty-Eight 925
description: While we built our business — and our passion — around vintage watches, it’s doubtful there’s a contemporary collection that better encapsulates the vintage spirit better than Tudor’s Black Bay. And more specifically, the Black Bay Fifty-Eight. The Black Bay Fifty-Eight takes all that we love about vintage dive watches — and more specifically, vintage Tudor and Rolex dive watches — and boils it down to an absurdly handsome, highly wearable modern timepiece. Originally launched in a black dial with a steel case, Tudor has since expanded the offerings to include offbeat metals such as bronze, solid gold, and even sterling silver! The particular Black Bay Fifty-Eight we have here, a Reference M79010SG-0001, features a 39mm sterling silver case with a sapphire crystal, a unidirectional rotating dive timing bezel with a grey insert, a luminous matte grey dial with a matching ‘snowflake’ handset, and a screw-down rose crown. Powered by the automatic, chronometer-certified Tudor MT5400 movement, it comes fitted to a signed brown leather strap with a signed sterling silver pin buckle. If you love the idea of a vintage Tudor or Rolex but don’t want to baby a vintage piece, then take it from us — the Black Bay Fifty-Eight is the watch to buy. This beautiful, unexpected execution offers something a bit out of the norm, and we love it.

title: Citizen Promaster Aqualand
description: Steeped in rich historical origins, Citizen Watch Company dates to 1924 when the Shokosha Watch Research Institute produced its first pocket watch, aptly named the "CITIZEN". Then-mayor of Tokyo, Mr. Shimpei Goto, named the watch "CITIZEN" with the hope that it, a luxury item in those days, would become widely available to ordinary citizens and be sold throughout the world. The rest is, as they say, history. Citizen has since evolved into one of the most widely available and popular affordable watch brands, whose innovative Eco-Drive technology harnesses natural and artificial light to power their Eco-Drive timepiece line. The Citizen Aqualand line, which launched in 1985, was the world’s first watch to incorporate an electronic, digital depth gauge, making for a dive watch unparalleled in its utility and sophistication. The particular Citizen we have here is an Aqualand Promaster Reference JP2007-09W - similar to the one seen on the wrist of French actor Jean Reno in the 1998 diving film Le Grand Bleu. Housed in a 50.7 x 51mm stainless steel case with mineral crystal, an unsigned crown, little barrel pushers, and a unidirectional, dive timing bezel, it features a luminous matte dial with black and white tone accents, a matching 'Aqualand' handset, and a digital, multi-function LED readout at 12:00. In excellent condition and powered by a quartz movement, it comes paired to a black rubber strap with matching hardware. It even includes its original Aqualand lock case packaging and hangtag! Of course, we love ourselves a good, ol’-fashioned mechanical dive watch here at Analog:Shift, but the Aqualand is part of dive watch history, beloved by professionals for its purpose-built design and no-nonsense aesthetic. And what could be cooler than that?

title: Tissot Squelette
description: Oftentimes in the hobby of watch collecting, there can be a bit too much focus on what the hot watch of the moment might be - the piece that no one can get, or the watch that everyone wants to own purely so they can say they own it. But in this frenzy of emphasis on what the next big windfall investment piece might be, there are literally tons of watches and brands which slip woefully under the radar. Tissot is among those brands. This particular Tissot is a modern beauty unlike anything else in the price category. A manually-wound skeleton, this piece has no real dial, with a complete openwork look into the industrial movement design architecture. The midcase is brushed, with a brushed bezel and polished case band. It's particularly exciting to see brands like Tissot investing in manually-wound movements. For a modernist look into traditional watchmaking, you'd be hard pressed to beat this piece at the price point! This one will go fast!

title: Patek Philippe Twenty-4
description: Patek Philippe may be known for its complicated calendar watches, but it’s equally renowned for the simple, elegant dress watches it makes for both men and women. Take the Twenty-4 line, for example: Released in 1999, this ladies’ collection is geared toward the modern women in search of a superlative timepiece that oozes elegance and class. Available in rectangular and round case profiles in various metals and configurations, it’s one of the best dedicated women’s watch collections on the market today. This particular Twenty-4, a Reference 7300/1200A-001 , is a modern piece from the current collection. Housed in a 36mm round stainless steel case with a diamond-set bezel and a signed, screw-down crown, it features a stunning blue sunburst dial with applied, luminous 'Arabic' indices, a lumed 'sword' handset, an open outer minute track, and a date window above 6 o'clock. Powered by the automatic Caliber 324 S C, it comes paired to a matching, polished stainless steel multi-link bracelet with a fold-over clasp. Accompanied by its factory goodies and papers, this beautiful Twenty-4 is a shining example of Patek Philippe's horological bona fides. Once you take it for a spin, you're sure to be as smitten with its charms as we are!

title: Patek Philippe Twenty-4
description: Patek Philippe may be known for its complicated calendar watches, but it’s equally renowned for the simple, elegant dress watches it makes for both men and women. Take the Twenty-4 line, for example: Released in 1999, this ladies’ collection is geared toward the modern women in search of a superlative timepiece that oozes elegance and class. Available in rectangular and round case profiles in various metals and configurations, it’s one of the best dedicated women’s watch collections on the market today. This particular Twenty-4, a Reference 4910/1G-001 from circa 2004, is a quietly expressive take on one of Patek Philippe’s most enduring designs. Housed in a 25mm rectangular case, it features polished brancards set with two elegant rows of diamonds, framing a soft cream dial with a matching handset. A Calatrava-signed crown and integrated multi-link bracelet complete the look, balancing refinement with everyday wearability. In great condition, showing light wear and powered by a reliable quartz E15 movement, this is a stunning timepiece that has to be seen to be believed.

title: Citizen Promaster Land
description: Steeped in rich historical origins, Citizen Watch Company dates to 1924 when the Shokosha Watch Research Institute produced its first pocket watch, aptly named the "CITIZEN". Then-mayor of Tokyo, Mr. Shimpei Goto, named the watch "CITIZEN" with the hope that it, a luxury item in those days, would become widely available to ordinary citizens and be sold throughout the world. The rest is, as they say, history. Citizen has since evolved into one of the most widely available and popular affordable watch brands, whose innovative Eco-Drive technology harnesses natural and artificial light to power their Eco-Drive timepiece line. The Citizen Aqualand line, which launched in 1985, was the world’s first watch to incorporate an electronic, digital depth gauge, making for a dive watch unparalleled in its utility and sophistication. The particular Citizen we have here is an outgrowth of the collection, a Promaster Land. This 44mm stainless steel case contains an Eco-Drive powered world time, chronograph, alarm, perpetual calendar, power save, charge level indicator, and summertime display. The functionality doesn't end there. This piece affords 200 meters of water resistance for hard use. In excellent condition, it comes paired to a black rubber strap with matching hardware. It even includes its original lock case packaging and hangtag! Of course, we love ourselves a good, ol’-fashioned mechanical dive watch here at Analog:Shift, but the Citizen Promaster Land is part of dive watch history, beloved by professionals for its purpose-built design and no-nonsense aesthetic. And what could be cooler than that?

title: Piaget Polo Two-Tone Diamonds
description: There’s nothing more classic and appealing than a simple dress watch. Though to be fair, when we say ‘simple’, we mean ‘uncomplicated’ — not unattractive! The 20th century saw a plethora of such watches hit the market. Generally available on leather straps and featuring two or three hands, these reliable wristwatches kept time during the classiest decades of the past 100 years. However, some were more innovative than others. Take this exquisite piece from Piaget, specialists in ultra-thin watchmaking: seamlessly machined such that there’s no perceivable break from watch head to bracelet, it’s as much akin to a piece of jewelry as it is to a more traditional wristwatch. Housed in a 34mm, yellow gold and white gold two tone alternating case with a fantastic, integrated bracelet with a signed clasp, this Polo features a two tone dial with brushed yellow gold components and bands of white gold diamond-set striping. You read that correctly — diamond ... set ... striping . Imagine the looks of curiosity — and envy — that you’ll stir up when rocking this bold Piaget at a cocktail party. With its eye-catching design, fascinating dial, and awesome bracelet, this is a standout timepiece appropriate for virtually any wrist. This piece could easily be mistaken for a bracelet with the design carrying straight onto the dial. That’s really part of the charm of so many of Piaget’s late 20th century watch designs. While these pieces went underappreciated for many years, we have recently seen a well-deserved surge in interest for these avant-garde designs.

title: Jaeger-LeCoultre Reverso
description: The Reverso is, in essence, one of the very first sports wristwatches. Despite its refined and elegant appearance, it combined utility with a sleek Art Deco exterior that has become an icon of horological design. But in the case of the Reverso, looks deceive. Though the model appears at first glance a dress watch, it owes its existence to the gentlemanly sport of polo. While touring India in the early 1930s, Swiss businessman César de Trey met a British polo player who'd just cracked the crystal of his watch on the field. De Trey returned to Switzerland with an idea already taken root in his head, which he then discussed with Jacques-David LeCoultre: What if a watch could be made with a case that could reverse? The reversible case, masterminded by designer Alfred Chauvot, was Jaeger-LeCoultre's solution to the problems polo players faced on the field. Once reversed, the delicate components of the case were protected from the shocks sustained while on horseback. No more would polo players crack the crystals or damage the dials of their watches. Chauvot took out a patent for the Reverso in March 1931. Produced chiefly in steel, Jaeger-LeCoultre also released the Reverso in gold, and decorated the case back with engravings or even miniature portraits. This particular Reverso, comes in with a 30mm stainless steel reversible case featuring an exhibition caseback on the reverse side. The front facing dial features a classic 'Arabic' layout with subsidiary seconds and a guilloche dial center. Powered by an in-house manually wound movement, this piece comes fitted to an alligator strap with a signed deployant clasp. In terms of elegant design and horological prowess, this watch punches way above its weight class, and simply cannot be overlooked.

title: Breitling Chronomat
description: Breitling has long been purveyor of chronographs to pilots, and the original Breitling Chronomat models of 1942 were built from the ground up to be no-nonsense instruments for use in the cockpit. The watch that you're looking at here is a contemporary Chronomat model that is equally no-nonsense! Dating to the 2020s, this Chronomat packs 200 meters of water resistance into the 44mm stainless steel case. Set with a polished steel bi-directional bezel, this piece possesses the classic Breitling case and bracelet paring of world renown. Its matte black dial conveys a plethora of information: An outer tachymeter scale and 1/5th-seconds track are joined by an outsized date window between 4 and 5 o'clock, applied luminous indices, and a triple-register chronograph display with running seconds, 12-hour, and 30-minute registers. A luminous 'sword' handset rounds out the feature set. Powered by the automatic Breitling B01 chronograph movement with a 70 hour power reserve, this hardy tool watch comes paired to its correct stainless steel multi-link bracelet with a signed folding clasp. Happily, it also includes its kit. In need of a serious everyday tool watch that you don't have to baby? Look no further!

title: Montblanc 1858 Chronograph
description: You might know Montblanc as a pen-maker, but that’s not the entire story. World famous for its writing instruments and leather goods, Montblanc began making watches in 1997. In 2006 Richemont — Montblanc’s parents company — purchased the historical Minerva manufacture, which Montblanc then took over in order to profit from its horological expertise. Since then, the maison has been churning out some of the finest modern timepieces available. The particular Montblanc we have here is a heritage callback to classic chronographs, albeit modernized for a contemporary audience. Sized 42mm in diameter, this Montblanc features an automatic winding movement and a beautiful retro display. The 'Arabic' dial features twin registers just like vintage Breguet chronos, and the slightly caked coloring of the handset and numerals reinforce the retro looks. If you love the aesthetics of vintage chronographs, and want something you're unlikely to ever see on another wrist, this beauty is an excellent option! Snag it while you can!

title: Vacheron Constantin Overseas Dual-Time
description: Just one look at the variety of multi-timezone watches available on the market (and across the price spectrum!) can make your head spin. From a value-packed Atlas GMT from humble microbrand Monta, to the Aquanaut ‘Travel Time’ from Patek Philippe , there are a slew of options that will serve as your trusty companion for travels abroad. O ne of our favorite luxury expressions of the travel watch is the Vacheron Constantin Overseas Dual-Time. This example, a Reference 7900V, dates to 2019 and features the Overseas' distinguishable case silhouette and integrated bracelet design with its barrel shaped midcase, polished, stylized bezel that subtly highlights the brand’s signature ‘Maltese cross’ and a screw-down date adjusting located at 4 o'clock. The stainless steel case measures 41mm in width and with its flat sapphire crystal, comes in a shade under 13mm in thickness, making for a damn good wearing experience. Its deep gloss blue dial alone is something worthy of admiration. Dynamic in all types of lighting, it features applied indices with polished luminous surrounds, a fully graduated rehaut , an AM/PM indicator at 9 o’clock, a recessed subsidiary date dial and a luminous ‘stick’ handset accompanied by an additional arrow hour hand to keep track of local time — which by the way features a a nifty function that allows it to sneak in under the main hour hand to keep the dial neat and tidy while you enjoy being grounded at home. It’s accompanied by a brilliantly designed - and infinitely comfortable - stainless steel bracelet with a signed deployant clasp. The Vacheron Constantin Dual-Time is already a modern classic, but the more intriguing thing about the model is that there’s still so much runway for the model to evolve. Maybe a regular production Overseas Dual-Time in titanium? Sign us up! But in the meantime, this 7900V with its fantastic blue dial is the best iteration yet and should absolutely not be missed.

title: Patek Philippe Nautilus
description: The Nautilus is without question one of the great horological icons of the 1970s, and has in recent years once again become one of the most in-demand timepiece models on the planet. Collectors and prospectors alike are clamoring to get their hands on Genta's second great luxury sports watch, and with the recent news of the discontinuation of their current-collection steel, time-and-date model — the legendary Reference 5711 — this dramatic rise shows no signs of slowing down. Interestingly, most demand for Nautilus models is centered around non-complicated models, something we can't fully wrap our heads around... This particular Nautilus, a Reference 5712/1A-001 from the the 2010s , comes in with a 40mm stainless steel case with a matching integrated bracelet and signed clasp, a sapphire crystal, a signed crown, and a brushed bezel with polished bevels . Inside, it features a gorgeous blue gradient 'teak' dial with applied luminous indices, a power reserve indicator, a moonphase aperture with date indicator, a small seconds register, and Patek Philippe's automatic winding Calibre 240 PS IRM C LU movement beating away within. The Nautilus has become a true piece of horological history; an icon of icons; and must be experienced to be understood!

title: Tudor Prince Date Chronograph
description: Tudor’s legacy as maker of high-quality tool watches was cemented long before the brand re-entered the American market in late 2013. Back in the 1990s and early 2000s, the brand was calling upon its legacy products to inspire a fresh crop of tool watches to satisfy the modern collector. These models function as the bridge between the classic Tudor watches of the 20th century and the modern reissues of the 21st. This Tudor Prince Date chronograph Ref. 79280 all of those boxes: Housed in a 40mm stainless steel Oyster case with screw-down chronograph pushers, a signed crown, a sapphire crystal, and a steel tachymeter bezel insert, it features a black dial with an outer 1/5th-seconds track, a triple-register chronograph display in black and white, a magnified date window at 3 o'clock, and a luminous 'baton' handset with a central chrono seconds. Powered by the automatic Valjoux 7750 chronograph movement and paired to a stainless steel multi-link bracelet with brushed and polished links and a signed clasp, this funky and approachable Tudor is the perfect entry point into the brand's collection. Wear it behind the wheel or walking down the busy city streets — just wear it well!

title: Breitling Superocean Automatic 36
description: Breitling has long been purveyor of chronographs to pilots, but in the 1950's, the budding popularity of dive watches such as the Rolex Submariner and the Blancpain Fifty Fathoms pushed it to enter that race. In 1957, the brand released both a time-only dive watch, the Superocean Reference 1004, and a chronograph in the form of the Reference 807. These pieces would form the beginning of a long-lasting and ever-evolving line of nautical watches beloved by many. The watch we’re offering here is a tremendously cool, contemporary take on the Superocean that started it all. Housed in an comfortable 36mm case with a polished, white ceramic timing bezel; a signed, screw-down crown; a sapphire crystal; and a signed, blue rubber dive strap with a matching folding clasp, it's a really comfortable everyday companion to add to your collection. The luminous, blue-white dial features quadrant 'baton' markers and 'square' plots with polished surrounds as well as a stylized, luminous handset with a square-tipped seconds hand. Its attention-grabbing attribute, however, lies within a large, white chapter ring that houses a fully graduated minute track. Powered by Breitling’s COSC-certified automatic Caliber 17, this piece is accompanied by its box, travel pouch, hangtag, and card. If you’ve been on the hunt for a robust diver with some everyday appeal, then this Breitling is the answer to your prayers!

title: Patek Philippe Ladies Cocktail Watch
description: Though the famous Calatrava is — for good reason — perhaps the best known of Patek Philippe’s incredible dress watches, there are myriad others that deserve recognition. From the rectangular Gondolo to the curvaceous Golden Ellipse, these timepieces, though simple in design, beg a closer look for their classic proportions and strict attention to detail. Patek has been producing such wristwatches for roughly a century of its 180-plus years, and shows no signs of slowing down. This particular timepiece is a Reference 3076 ladies' cocktail watch from the 1950s — a time when these thin, often diamond-encrusted pieces were still highly in vogue. Measuring approximately 14mm wide in a barrel-shaped white gold case, it features a smooth high polish bezel, an unsigned crown, and an acrylic crystal. Its simple silvered dial with applied 'Arabic' and 'dart' indices is joined by a matching 'pencil' handset, while a spectacular integrated bracelet is joined to the case with diamond-set lugs. Breathtaking ! Powered by a hand-wound movement, this magnificent cocktail watch is ready to transport its next wearer back to the prime of midcentury elegance.

title: Jaeger-LeCoultre Master Control Perpetual Calendar
description: From its inception and to the present day, Jaeger-LeCoultre has produced timepieces that are both timeless and perpetually appealing. Though their quality is often under-sung, JLC's present-day pieces sing to us in the same soft, alluring timbre as their vintage offerings. With understated elegance, they exude an appeal that even the most steadfast of vintage collectors can't resist. For a modern watch, the Master Control Perpetual Calendar looks as though it could be fifty years old, and we mean that in a good way — a very good way. With its slim 36.5mm stainless steel case and subregisters with month, day, date, and moon-phase indicators arranged artfully on the dial, it features many of the beloved design elements of some of our favorite midcentury wristwatches. With its applied 'dart' indices, skeletonized 'dauphine' handset, luminous 'dot' indices, and subtle moonphase display, it’s about as understated as a perpetual calendar can get. It has the look and feel of an older watch, but with the technical excellence and dependability that distinguishes their modern products. This watch is powered by the elegant Caliber 889/440/2 movement — JLC, as a true manufacture, designs and manufactures such movements to meet rigorous quality standards. Each calibre produced by the maison must pass a 1,000-hour test before leaving the factory. This test, more punishing than even those imposed by COSC, is implemented only by JLC. The Master Control series — which was joined in the mid 2000s by the Master Ultra Thin series — exudes the brilliance of modern design coupled with the emotional connection only a brand with the gravitas and wherewithal of Jaeger-LeCoultre could pull off. This particular Master Control Perpetual rests in a comfortable realm between luxury and practicality, at home in both casual and professional environments. It's the perfect combination of vintage elegance and modern ingenuity, a testament to the gravitas with which JLC imbues all of its timepieces.

title: Breitling Endurance Pro 44 Chronograph 'IRONMAN'
description: Breitling has long been purveyor of chronographs to pilots, having designed models such as 1942’s Chronomat from the ground as a no-nonsense instrument for use in the cockpit. Since then, numerous Breitling chronographs have seen action in myriad scenarios, not the least of which is on the wrists of athletes and ordinary folks simply going about their business, who need a strong, reliable watch to time everyday events. The Breitling Endurance Pro in particular stands out for its versatile use cases, being equally at home out in the field or simply out for a stroll. This particular Endurance Pro from the 2020s features a COSC-certified quartz movement caliber 82— as if the common battery-powered caliber wasn’t accurate enough, Breitling did you once better! Housed in a 44mm polymer Breighlight case with a sapphire crystal, a signed crown, 'piston' pushers, and a black polymer, bi-directional 'compass' bezel, it features a luminous red fume dial with applied 'Arabic' indices, a matching handset, a triple-register chronograph with a date window at 4:30, and an inner pulsometer scale around the dial periphery. This piece is part of the IRONMAN special edition collection, coming specifically in the red colorway. Powered by the Caliber Breitling 82 thermocompensated SuperQuartz movement and paired to a red rubber strap with a black polymer pin buckle, the Reference X823109A1K1S1 is ready for a lifetime of adventure with its next owner. That owner should be you!

title: Tiffany & Co. Diamond Cocktail Watch
description: From the 1920s through the 1940s, small ladies’ cocktail watches were all the rage. These delicate timepieces — thin, often crafted in precious metals and paired with integrated bracelets — were designed as much as jewelry as they were instruments for telling time. With their diminutive dials and finely crafted movements, they offered elegance without excess, making them the perfect companions for evenings out and formal occasions. This particular example, retailed by Tiffany & Co., captures that spirit beautifully. The petite, round platinum case is richly set with diamonds, extending seamlessly into a refined, 'tennis-style' bracelet that wraps the wrist in light. The design feels distinctly mid-century, with subtle Art Deco undertones in its symmetry and geometry, yet remains effortlessly wearable today. The dial provides a quiet counterpoint to the brilliance surrounding it. Softly patinated and restrained, it features 'Arabic' numerals, a delicate minute track, and slender 'Breguet-style' hands that keep the focus on clarity and proportion. It’s a thoughtful balance, with just enough detail to ground the watch without competing with the case's sparkle. Light, graceful, and unapologetically elegant, it reflects a time when watches were designed to accompany candlelight, champagne, and the understated glamour of an evening out on the town.

title: IWC Portugieser Hand-Wound Eight Days 75th Anniversary
description: The Portugieser was so-called because two IWC clients from Portugal approached the manufacture with a somewhat daunting task: to produce a wristwatch that was just as accurate as a marine chronometer. In those days, the only way to ensure that degree of accuracy in a wristwatch would be to cannibalize a movement from a pocket watch and put it in a wristwatch case. The watches the resulted from such a union were large, a huge departure from the slim cases that typified wristwatches of the era. So that’s what IWC did, using a Hunter Calibre 74 pocket watch movement to create the first Portugieser. In 2015, IWC released the watch that we have here, the Reference 5102 Portuguieser made to celebrate the 75th anniversary of the original Portuguieser which debuted in 1939. This watch comes in at the same size as the original too, at 43mm wide. At the time, the standard size of most men's watches was closer to 33mm or 34mm, so while this watch isn't exactly small now, it would have been absolutely massive 75 years ago! This watch features a gorgeous matte black dial with printed 'Arabic' quadrant numerals, a subsidiary seconds counter at the 6:00 position, and an exhibition caseback, displaying the IWC Calibre 59215 hand-cranking movement with a massive 8-day power reserve. With only 750 examples made in steel, this is an excellent opportunity to pick up a contemporary classic with its full kit, and one of IWC's best releases in recent years!

title: Longines 'Slim' Tank
description: There’s a particular kind of confidence in doing less, and doing it exceptionally well. Longines has long understood this balance, and pieces like this are a perfect expression of that restraint. Rather than relying on ornament or complication, it leans fully into proportion, surface, and line — the quiet fundamentals that define truly enduring design. Housed in an elongated rectangular 18K yellow gold case, this ultra-slim “Tank” silhouette feels almost architectural on the wrist. The brushed gold dial continues that theme, stripped down to its essence with only a subtle 'crosshair' layout and a simple handset. No numerals, no excess — just a study in symmetry and tone that shifts beautifully with the light. Powered by a manual-wind movement, it stays true to traditional watchmaking while maintaining an almost jewelry-like presence. Paired with a rose-colored alligator strap, the warmth of the gold case is amplified, giving the watch an understated yet unmistakably elegant character.

title: Breguet Classique
description: There are few names in watchmaking that carry the same quiet authority as Breguet — and even fewer that can claim to have shaped the very language of modern horology. Founded in 1775 by Abraham-Louis Breguet, the maison is responsible for some of the most important inventions in watchmaking history, including the tourbillon, the Breguet overcoil, and the 'pare-chute' shock protection system. Just as influential, however, is the brand’s design vocabulary: engine-turned guilloché dials, elegant 'Roman' numerals, and those unmistakable blued 'Breguet' hands. These weren’t just aesthetic choices — they were innovations in legibility and refinement, developed for an elite clientele that included royalty, scientists, and statesmen. That balance of technical mastery and visual restraint remains at the core of Breguet today. This Ladies’ Classique Ref. 8561 is a perfect expression of that enduring philosophy. Crafted in 18K white gold, the 25.5mm case is elegantly framed by a diamond-set bezel and lugs, adding brilliance without disrupting the watch’s elegance. Beneath the sapphire crystal sits Breguet’s signature silvered guilloché dial that is engine-turned by hand, individually finished, and endlessly captivating as it plays with the light. Blued steel hands sweep across 'Roman' numerals with that unmistakable Breguet grace, while a signed crown, marked with the maison’s “B,” completes the overall design. Powered by a manual-wind movement and paired with a complementary burgundy alligator strap, it wears with quiet ceremony and style. Understated, intricate, and deeply rooted in tradition, this is Breguet, not simply a manufacture, but a living blueprint for what fine watchmaking can be.

title: Enigma Ladies Sports Watch
description: Gianni Bulgari, a watch designer and third-generation member of the famed Greek-Italian jewelry dynasty, feels that women's sports watches should be just as compelling as men's pieces. In 1989, following the sale of his stake in the Bulgari company to his family, he established a new watchmaking firm called GB Enigma in Switzerland to design incredible timepieces with unusual designs. This particular Enigma, an integrated strap sports piece, is emblematic of the firm's bold design philosophy: Housed in a 27mm 18K gold case with an integrated leather strap with matching pin buckle, this piece is completed with a black cabochon crown, a tapisserie dial with applied lume plots a skeletonized 'sword' handset, and a three o'clock date window. All told, this piece would make a great everyday watch. A lesser known brand with a powerful pedigree, an excellent design, and a practical package for everyday applications, don't let this piece slide past you! It's also a great value!

title: IWC Pilot's Watch Mark XVI
description: The IWC Pilot's Watch can trace its history back to the oversized timepieces produced for military aviators in 1940. These enormous pieces were originally designed for maximum legibility in a cockpit setting, and — believe it or not — were meant to be worn on the pilot's thigh! Today, the Pilot's line is perhaps IWC's most identifiable collection, featuring a variety of case and dial configurations suited for any taste — provided your wrist can handle the larger-than-average dimensions. If not, that’s where the Mark series comes in. These watches are contemporary takes on the original Mark X and Mark XI watches from the 1940s. This particular watch, a Pilot's Watch MARK XVI, features a 39mm brushed stainless steel case with a sapphire crystal, a signed crown, and a polished, stepped bezel. It houses a luminous matte black dial with printed ‘Arabic’ indices, a matching ‘sword’ handset, and IWC's Calibre 30110 with 21 jewels and a 41-hour power reserve within. Paired to a beautiful multi-link bracelet, this piece is the essence of a Pilot's watch. With excellent legibility, handsome looks, and utilitarian, military influence, it’s no surprise that the Mark series has become a cornerstone of the IWC collection.

title: Tudor Pelagos
description: In the past 10 years, Tudor has been doing a killer job with its contemporary offerings — especially in the tool watch department. Timepieces from their Black Bay, Heritage Chronograph, and Ranger collections have been flying off the shelves for their impressive looks, sporty construction and aggressive price points. But for sheer utility, sportiness, and integration of modern materials, it’s the Pelagos line that has us most impressed. Though it draws from Tudor’s back catalog of dive watches for aesthetic inspiration, the Pelagos brings a forward-thinking design to the professional-grade watch arena. Constructed from titanium and featuring a unique automatically-adjusting clasp system for their bracelet models, this is the type of timepiece that a saturation diver could happily strap on while working hundreds of meters below the surface. And more recently, we’ve seen the Pelagos collection make a 10/10 splash with the introduction of the Pelagos 39, whose reduced proportions make the dive watch even more appealing to a casual wearer. This particular Pelagos, a Reference M25600TB-0001, represents one of the first building blocks for the collection and features a 42mm titanium case with a helium escape valve, sapphire crystal, signed crown, and a unidirectional rotating dive timing bezel with a luminous blue insert. It features a bright blue dial with luminous indices, an outer minute track, date window at 3:00, and a ‘snowflake’ handset. Paired to an excellent multi-link titanium bracelet with the brand's proprietary self-adjusting clasp, this piece is powered by the in-house, COSC-certified Tudor Calibre MT5612 automatic movement with a 70-hour power reserve. We can’t state this clearly enough: The Pelagos is one of the best dive watch designs of the 21st century — and very well may be the most sensible choice from the Rolex family for professional divers. It is nothing short of a modern classic.

title: Patek Philippe Annual Calendar Flyback Chronograph
description: The annual calendar is a complication invented by Patek Philippe. While it plays second fiddle to the perpetual calendar in the minds of most collectors, the reality is that the construction of this calendar mechanism is by no means a simple feat, and requires impressive micro-engineering and design acumen to execute well. Able to account for months of different lengths, it only requires manual adjustment once per year — at the end of February. However, this is no ordinary annual calendar: A Reference 5905P-010 from 2021, it also includes a flyback chronograph — one in which a single button press both stops and instantaneously restarts the chronograph. Convenient for timing laps, this complication proves unbeatable when combined with the utility of an annual calendar. This particular 5905P-010 is housed in a 42mm platinum case with a signed crown, barrel pushers, a polished, concave bezel, and a sapphire crystal. It features a luminous glossy dark dial with applied indices, a white minute track with a radial subsidiary register, a matching luminous dauphine handset, date, month, and day apertures, and a running seconds sub-register above 6 o’clock. Flip the watch over, and one is greeted — via a sapphire exhibition caseback — with a view of the automatic Patek Philippe Calibre CH 28-520 QA 24H movement. Paired to a 21mm, signed black alligator leather strap with a signed, platinum pin buckle, this is simply a stunning timepiece in every regard: horologically, aesthetically, and functionally. If you’re the type of collector who only has eyes for the best, then this watch is for you!

title: Lucien Piccard Ladies Diamond 'Cocktail' Watch
description: In the 1950s and 1960s, the cocktail watch wasn’t just an accessory, it was part of the occasion. These pieces were designed to disappear into the rhythm of an evening, catching light rather than attention, pairing effortlessly with silk, champagne, and late hours. Small in scale but often extravagant in execution, they balanced mechanical ingenuity with jewelry-level artistry in a way that feels increasingly rare today. This particular example from Lucien Piccard captures that spirit beautifully. Founded in 1923 in Lausanne, the brand built its reputation on elegant, refined dress watches, and this piece leans fully into that heritage. The petite dial sits almost like a secret at the center of a fully diamond-set case, framed by brilliant stones that extend seamlessly into a 'tennis' style bracelet. Tapered 'baguette' accents add structure and geometry, giving the piece just enough architectural edge to balance the sparkle. Look closer, and the charm deepens. The signed crown features Piccard’s signature flourish — a subtle, almost whimsical curlicue that feels delightfully out of step with the otherwise formal design. It’s a small detail, but exactly the kind that gives a watch personality. Finished with a box clasp and safety latch, the diamond 'tennis' style bracelet wears as fluidly as it looks. Light, intricate, and undeniably glamorous, it’s a reminder of a time when watches were made not just to tell time, but to celebrate it.

title: Heuer Calculator Automatic Chronograph
description: There is perhaps no more versatile complication than the chronograph. From race cars to rocket ships, boats to battlefields, you are likely to find a chronograph being employed to measure a plethora of tasks and times. From the Greek khrónos (“time) and gráphō (“to write”), a chronograph is a watch that has the ability to record time, generally via the addition of small sub-counters that register minutes and hours. Though many storied brands produced notable chronographs during the complication’s heyday from the 1930s through the 1970s, numerous other firms fell by the wayside during the Quartz Crisis, folding completely and disappearing or being rolled up into conglomerates that pillaged their stores of parts. Heuer, for its part, is still around today as TAG Heuer, but its pre-TAG period was perhaps its most fertile era of chronograph design. Take the Calculator watch, for example: this special model took advantage of the brand's esteemed automatic chronograph movement technology and paired it with a unique two-part slide rule bezel in what can best be described as a sort of mechanical calculator: Using the bezel, the wearer can easily calculate distance and weight and convert between metric and imperial. Pretty neat! This particular Heuer Calculator watch, a Reference 110.633, dates to the 1970s and features a 45.5mm 'tonneau-shaped' stainless steel case with an outer calculator bezel, dual 'pump' pushers, a left-hand crown, and a stainless steel multi-link bracelet. Its dial is a picture of legibility: Featuring an outer 1/5th-seconds track in white, luminous 'dash' indices, a dual-register chronograph with 30-minute and 12-hour counters, a date window at 6 o'clock, and a luminous orange 'baton' handset, it's easy to use as a time-recording device or for more complex calculations. Powered by the Heuer Calibre 12 automatic chronograph movement and paired to a cool stainless steel multi-link bracelet, this unique tool watch is an awesome vestige of one of the most creative periods in chronograph design. It brings a smile to our faces every time — we're confident that it'll do the same for you.

title: Cartier Baignoire
description: Ladies' watches, unfortunately, don’t get the attention they deserve. It has seemingly been long assumed that women will be content with shoddily-designed timepieces devoid of soul, providing of course that they are pink and have loads of precious stones set on them. We know this to be nonsense — a lady’s watch shouldn't be any less considered than a man’s, and we seek out examples of watches done right, regardless of intended gender. At Analog:Shift HQ, we are excited to curate a selection of timepieces that provide equally engaging options for ladies as the more common men’s pieces. No watch better captures this philosophy than the Cartier Baignoire. This example, a Reference W15045D8-1954 , features a beautiful case shape, with its soft curves and oblong 'ellipse' aesthetic. Paired with a beautiful 18K yellow gold bracelet with an equally distinct link profile, this watch is anything but boring, and provides an incredible host of details to obsess over. In spite of the unique design, this piece still preserves many of the details of classic Cartier we would hope to see: a 'Roman' numeral dial, blued steel hands, petite Cartier logo, and more all serve as a reminder that this piece is backed by a name with an unrivaled history for fine jewelry making, and yes, watchmaking too. A Baignoire on a leather strap is a solid choice for any occasion. However, a Baignoire on an integrated bracelet is seemingly more our style!

title: Patek Philippe Calatrava
description: Patek Philippe has long been known for its complicated watchmaking, but following the stock market crash of 1929, fewer clients had the means to purchase these high-end timepieces. After securing a controlling interest in the firm, Jean and Charles Henri Stern decided to create a simple watch that would come in at a lower price point and appeal to the firm’s clientele. Using the principles espoused by the Bauhaus school and its minimalist, function-first philosophy, Patek conceived the Calatrava, taking its name from the Spanish military order established in the 12th century which had become the symbol of Patek Philippe. Since its debut in 1932, the Calatrava has become a byword for a simple, time-only dress watch — even from brands other than Patek itself. This piece, a Reference 6007G-011, isn’t your run-of-the-mill Calatrava. Debuted in 2023 in three individual colorways — red, blue and yellow — this particular Calatrava features an array of automotive-eqsque design cues that easily makes it the sportiest 'Trava in their modern lineup. Housed in a 40mm 18K white gold case, this piece features a polished bezel and case complemented by a sapphire crystal and a signed crown. The black luminous dial features a matching white gold handset and radially configured, luminous, white gold Arabic indices. Straying away from the traditional dressier aesthetics of the Calatrava model, the Patek Philippe design team elected to add sportier design notes with its center dial adorned with a 'carbon' finish and a blue accented, checker-style, minute track. Outfitted to a signed black leather strap with a matching 'carbon' embossing, contrast blue stitching and a spade-style 18K white gold pin buckle, this piece comes with its matching kit. Traditional Calatrava aesthetics were thrown out the window on this one and we're here for it!

title: Seiko Speedtimer SRQ057 'Datsun'
description: Seiko makes fantastic watches. Pure and simple. From the rare as hen's teeth Hi-Beat 6159 to the film-famous 6105 and 6309 models to the economy workhorse SXK007, Japanese watchmakers perfected incredibly well-made tool watches decades ago - and at prices that make the most diehard Swiss watch fans question their spending habits. This piece, a Reference SRQ057 , dates to 2025 and recalls one of the coolest pieces of Seiko history. In 1971, a Seiko Datsun was on the wrist of number 11 when he won the East-African Safari Rally, one of the most harrowing races in history. The incredible chrono we have here is a tribute limited edition of the Datsun, sporting the advantages of modern watchmaking including sapphire crystals and an updated automatic movement. The piece measures 42mm in diameter and features an automatic movement within. Built on a reputation for fine chronograph construction, this Seiko is one to save for the ages, recalling one of the coolest moves in Seiko history. These are wonderful timepieces that represent just about everything that we love about Seiko and are overlooked by many. Don't make that mistake!

title: Cartier Tank Americaine
description: The Cartier Tank Americaine is quite possibly the least understood model in Cartier's lineup. Introduced in 1989, it is, in many ways, a modern update to the classic Cintrée. With an elongated case featuring a gently curved caseback, the collection was designed from the start to be a larger watch to suit American tastes. Initially launched exclusively with quartz movements, it has expanded in the past decades to include a variety of mechanical variants as well as sizes, case materials, and complications that offers something appealing to a wide range of collectors. This particular example is a beautiful ladies' Reference W2620031-2503 cased in 18K rose gold, and features the timeless satin silver guilloché dial with printed 'Roman' indices upon which Cartier was built. Measuring 19mm x 34.8 mm , it boasts smooth brancards , while a sapphire-set cabochon crown and a sapphire crystal complete the picture of a supremely elegant case. The silver guilloché dial — fitted with an inner 'railroad' minute track and blued steel 'sword' handset — is a straightforward picture of pure class. Meanwhile, a handy quartz movement and a stunning scale-link bracelet in 18K rose gold with a hidden butterfly clasp seal the deal. The Tank Louis is all well and good. But for our money, this incredible, elongated Americaine is equally deserving of your attention!

title: Breitling Super Avenger B01 Chronograph 42
description: Breitling has long been purveyor of chronographs to pilots. In fact, the original Breitling Chronomat models of the 1940s were built from the ground up to be no-nonsense instruments for use in the cockpit. Since then, Breitling has expanded on its line of aviation watches, moving into more mechanically (and visually) complex chronographs, as well as more straightforward, legible designs such as the Avenger line. This particular piece, an Avenger B01 42 features a — you guessed it — 42mm black ceramic case with dual 'paddle' pushers, a sapphire crystal, a signed crown, and a unique dive bezel with signature 'rider tabs'. Inside, it has a lovely light blue dial with applied luminous 'Arabic' indices, dark blue chronograph registers, an outer minute track, and a luminous 'sword' handset. Paired to a light blue leather strap with a signed titanium deployant clasp, it's powered by the COSC-certified automatic Calibre B01 movement from Breitling. Equipped with 45 jewels and a 70-hour power reserve, this workhorse movement is ready to take on any adventure — just flip the watch over and you'll receive confirmation that it's working overtime via a sapphire caseback. If you’ve been on the hunt for an excellent, serious tool watch watch with some heft, you've found it. What's more, it's from one of the best brands in the game!

title: Movado 'Calatrava' Field Watch
description: Never underestimate the power of a field-style Movado watch.
