Especially one from their mid-century output, a period when the brand was quietly producing some of the most thoughtful, well-balanced designs in Swiss watchmaking. Not loud, not flashy, just consistently right. This 1950s Movado Ref. 18126 is a perfect example. Housed in a 34mm stainless steel case with faceted lugs and an acrylic crystal, it strikes that ideal balance between durability and elegance. It wears like a field watch, compact, legible, and practical, but carries the refinement of a proper dress piece. The dial is where it really comes together. A warm champagne tone, aged into a rich, almost salmon hue, gives the watch a sense of depth and character that only time can produce. Applied 'Arabic' numerals and 'diamond' markers keep things highly legible, while the 'syringe' hands add a graphic strength to the dial. At six o’clock, a recessed sub-seconds register anchors the layout, adding symmetry without clutter. Powered by a hand-wound movement, it’s everything you need, nothing you don’t. A versatile vintage piece that feels just as right today as it did the day it left the factory. The kind of watch that doesn’t try to impress, yet ends up doing exactly that!

title: Benrus Sky Chief
description: Early Pilot Chronographs are straight up cool. Regardless of the name on the dial, these watches from the 50s easily catch our attention. In the 1940s, watches made by Benrus were flying off the shelves… literally. And among them, the best-known is this watch: the Sky Chief, perhaps one of the most perfect pilot’s watches ever made. The Sky Chief, introduced in 1940, is without a doubt the brand’s most famous chronograph. Though it was released in several variations (including triple-date models), it’s the pilot’s version that’s become the most iconic. At first glance, it’s a tool watch, looking much like the chronographs worn by the world’s air forces. This Benrus Sky Chief features a heavily patinated three register silver-tone dial with a coin-edged finish to the bezel. The bi-directional rotating bezel features a tiny red timing indicator, a small but important detail that so many of these mid-century chronographs feature. Elongated blued steel hands stretch across the dial as well as fill the sub-dials. This piece represents an important element of the watch industry of the 50s, an industry that endeavored to build capable tools for a purpose. If you're into adding unique vintage chronographs to your collection that don't break the bank, this Benrus might just be the perfect solution.

title: Cartier Tank Americaine Automatic Diamonds
description: The Cartier Tank Americaine is quite possibly the least understood model in Cartier's lineup. Introduced in 1989, it is, in many ways, a modern update to the classic Cintrée. With an elongated case featuring a gently curved caseback, the collection was designed from the start to be a larger watch to suit American tastes. Initially launched exclusively with quartz movements, it has expanded in the past decades to include a variety of mechanical variants as well as sizes, case materials, and complications that offers something appealing to a wide range of collectors. This particular example is a beautiful Reference WB710010-2504 cased in 18K rose gold, and features the timeless satin silver guilloché dial with printed 'Roman' indices upon which Cartier was built. Measuring 41.6 x 22.6 mm and 9.6mm tall, its brancards feature dual rows of 'brilliant-cut' diamonds, while a diamond-set cabochon crown and a sapphire crystal complete the picture of a supremely elegant case. The silver guilloché dial — fitted with an inner 'railroad' minute track, a blued steel 'sword' handset, and a date window above 6 o'clock — is a straightforward picture of pure class. Meanwhile, an automatic Calibre 077 movement and a stunning scale-link bracelet in 18K rose gold with a hidden butterfly clasp seal the deal. Forget about the Tank Louis for a moment, and spring for something with a touch of sparkle!

title: Royce Super Compressor Diver
description: One of the most common requests we get are for affordable vintage super compressors, and it's truly no surprise to us. Super compressor watches, noted for their unique water proofing system and recognizable twin-crown display, these peices have an incredible degree of cool and pack vintage charm to the nines. If you're here, you know we have a particular affection for off-brand timepieces- there are an untold number of fantastic watches that combine sharp looks, robust build quality, and time-tested movements just as well as the bigger names at a fraction of the price. If you've never heard of Royce before, we wouldn't blame you. While the brand dates back to the 1920's, they spent decades turning out simple, reliable watches with high-grade cases and movements before falling victim to the quartz crisis like so many other brands. This particular piece is an example of Royce at its best. An Incabloc dial, black display, and date at 3 o'clock, this watch looks much like JLC dive watches, and many of the more expensive vintage divers we love to collect. Equipped with a super compressor case, cross-hatch engraved twin crowns, an internal rotating bezel and one of our vibrant Analog:Shift leather straps, this piece gives you a window into that world at a fraction of the cost and with lots of mechanical and vintage intrigue to fall back on. Even with all of this, the obscurity of the Royce name and history makes most un-illuminated enthusiasts breeze right past it. But you know better. That's why you're here.

title: Breitling SuperOcean Heritage Chronograph 44
description: Breitling may be best known for its aviation roots, but by the late 1950s, the brand had its sights set below the surface. As dive watches surged in popularity, led by icons like the Submariner and Fifty Fathoms, Breitling answered with the SuperOcean in 1957, introducing both a time-only diver and a chronograph built for underwater use. It marked the beginning of a nautical lineage that remains central to the brand today. This SuperOcean Heritage Chronograph 44 carries that legacy forward with a modern standpoint. Housed in a 44mm stainless steel case, the watch balances presence with purpose. The unidirectional timing bezel and screw-down architecture reinforce its dive-ready credentials, while the sapphire crystal, solid caseback, and signed crown speak to everyday durability. The dark sunburst blue dial adds depth and personality — shifting tones in the light — while applied indices with outer lume plots and Breitling’s signature hands ensure clarity in any condition. A 'day-and-date' display adds practical functionality without overwhelming the design. Inside beats the Breitling Caliber 13, a COSC-certified automatic chronograph movement known for its reliability and ease of service. It’s paired here with a blue 'mesh' rubber strap, giving the watch a more contemporary, sport-driven feel on the wrist. Offered as a complete set with its box, warranty card, and hangtag, this example checks all the right boxes for a modern tool watch with heritage appeal.

title: Patek Philippe Ladies Annual Calendar Moonphase Diamonds
description: There are complicated watches, and then there are complicated watches that never forget they’re meant to be beautiful. This Patek Philippe Annual Calendar Moon Phase reference 4937G-001 sits firmly in that second category. At 37mm in white gold, the case strikes a refined balance on the wrist, with proportions that are elegant and considered. What sets it apart immediately, however, is the diamond setting. 472 stones totaling approximately 3.23 carats, carefully arranged across the bezel and lugs. It’s a substantial presence, but handled with the kind of restraint and taste that defines Patek at its best. The dial brings a different kind of richness. Rendered in luminous mother-of-pearl, it shifts gently in tone as the light moves across its surface, giving the watch a soft, almost ethereal quality. The layout remains clear and purposeful: day and month are displayed in twin subdials, with a moon-phase aperture above six o’clock and a date window below. Applied Arabic numerals and elegant hands keep everything legible, even as the dial plays with texture and light. Inside is one of Patek Philippe’s most practical complications — the annual calendar. Designed to account for months of 30 and 31 days, it requires adjustment just once per year, blending mechanical ingenuity with everyday usability in a way few complications manage so effortlessly. Paired with a matching strap and a diamond-set buckle, this example is offered with extras, including its inner and outer boxes and a Certificate of Origin. It’s a piece that balances technical depth with visual allure, a watch that feels as considered in its mechanics as it does in its design...and SPARKLE. A reminder that at Patek Philippe, complexity is never at odds with elegance — it’s simply another way of expressing it.

title: Breitling Endurance Chronograph
description: Breitling has long been purveyor of chronographs to pilots, having designed models such as 1942’s Chronomat from the ground as a no-nonsense instrument for use in the cockpit. Since then, numerous Breitling chronographs have seen action in myriad scenarios, not the least of which is on the wrists of athletes and ordinary folks simply going about their business, who need a strong, reliable watch to time everyday events. The Breitling Endurance Pro in particular stands out for its versatile use cases, being equally at home out in the field or simply out for a stroll. This particular Endurance Pro from the 2020s features a COSC-certified quartz movement Caliber 82— as if the common battery-powered caliber wasn’t accurate enough, Breitling did you once better! Housed in a 44mm polymer 'Breighlight' case with a sapphire crystal, a signed crown wrapped in red rubber, 'piston' pushers, and a black polymer, bi-directional compass bezel, it features a luminous matte black dial with applied 'Arabic' indices, a matching handset, a triple-register chronograph with a date window at 4:30, and an inner pulsometer scale around the dial periphery. Powered by the Caliber Breitling 82 thermocompensated SuperQuartz movement and paired to a red rubber strap with a black polymer pin buckle, the Reference X82310D91B1S1 is ready for a lifetime of adventure with its next owner. That owner should be you!

title: Cartier Tank Mini Diamonds 'MOP'
description: When you think of Cartier, their longstanding history in jewelry probably comes to mind. But they also have a reputation for making timeless and elegant timepieces, which they’ve been doing for over a century. The Tank Louis is without question the brand’s most well-known model, but the story doesn't end there. Beyond the Louis line are numerous executions — many produced in very limited quantities — that are quite literally some of the most beautiful timepieces ever made. This particular Tank is an extraordinary ladies’ piece from circa the 1990s. It features a striking 15.5mm x 25mm white gold case - set with diamonds on the vertical 'brancards', a mineral crystal, a sapphire cabochon crown, and a stunning mother of pearl dial with a matching 'sword' handset. To top it all off, this piece is fitted to a 18K white gold bracelet with a beads of rice profile. Outstanding. One could spend an entire lifetime just collecting Tanks, nevermind the rest of the staggering Cartier oeuvre . Why not start here, with this rare, stunning piece that you are not likely to catch again. Get this one before it's too late!

title: Cartier Panthère Diamonds
description: Cartier is responsible for some of the most recognizable and successful wristwatches in the history of modern horology. The definitive expert on watch design, the French maison has always been a leader in challenging conventional approaches to watchmaking while pioneering new styling as a fixture in the broader fashion world. Over the last hundred years or so, Cartier has introduced some of the most recognizable watch designs in history, from the Tank to the Santos to the Crash. In the realm of ladies’ watches, few pieces possess the elegance and timeless appeal of the Panthère. The Panthère has a history intimately intertwined with Cartier's. During the First World War, the Parisian maison designed a ladies’ wristwatch featuring a spotted diamond-and-onyx pattern. Commissioned by Louis Cartier, it was a gift to Belgian-French jeweler Jeanne Toussaint, whom he referred to as ‘La Panthère.’ Over a century later, the Panthère line is still a fixture in the Cartier catalog, and the big cat motif appears frequently on watches, jewelry, and more. This particular Panthère de Cartier is a ladies’ staple, fashioned in white gold with a gorgeous, correct 18K white gold bracelet. This piece features a diamond bezel, a diamond-set bracelet and case, and a white ‘Roman’ dial topped with blued steel hands. This piece is powered by a quartz movement, befitting a jewelry-forward piece like this one. This watch is elegance personified in white gold. It is truly the most elevated expression of Cartier for women, and would serve as an accent in a seasoned collection, or as a token dress watch for important events.

title: Breitling Avenger B01 Chronograph 42
description: Breitling has long been purveyor of chronographs to pilots, and the original Breitling Chronomat models of 1942 were built from the ground up to be no-nonsense instruments for use in the cockpit. Since then, Breitling has expanded on their line of aviation watches, moving into more mechanically (and visually) complex chronographs, as well as more straightforward, legible designs such as the Avenger line. This particular piece, an Avenger B01 42, features — you guessed it — a 42mm stainless steel case with a sapphire crystal, a signed crown, and a unique dive bezel. Inside, it has a blue dial with applied luminous indices and a matching handset. Powered by a COSC-certified automatic Calibre B01 movement from Breitling, it comes paired with the matching stainless steel bracelet with deployant buckle hiding an on-the-fly micro adjustment. Original box, travel pouch, warranty card, and hang tags are also included. If you’ve been on the hunt for an excellent, serious tool watch watch with some heft, you've found it. Once more, it's from one of the best brands in the game.

title: Breitling Avenger Automatic 42
description: If you think Breitling is a one-trick pony, it might be time to look a little closer. Founded in 1884, the brand has spent well over a century building some of the most capable aviation watches ever made. But beyond the slide rules and cockpit timers, Breitling has always excelled at something more fundamental: making watches that work. Robust, legible, and easy to service, they’re the horological equivalent of a well-built tool — dependable, no-nonsense, and ready for whatever you throw at them. The Avenger line takes that ethos and pushes it further. Designed with jet pilots in mind, it’s less about nostalgia and more about capability — a modern interpretation of what an aviation watch should be today. This Reference A17328101B1A1, sized at 42mm, delivers exactly that. The black dial is clean and highly legible, framed by a unidirectional rotating bezel with deep, easy-grip knurling. Substantial crown guards, a screw-down crown, and thick, purposeful lugs reinforce the watch’s tool-first mentality. Paired with a steel bracelet, the overall look is tactical without feeling overbuilt. With 300 meters of water resistance, corrosion-resistant construction, and intuitive handling even with gloves, the Avenger is designed to perform in extreme conditions — whether that’s a cockpit, a coastline, or somewhere in between. You don’t need a flight suit to wear one. But it doesn’t hurt.

title: Movado Acvatic Dress Watch
description: At a glance, this looks like a simple mid-century dress watch. It isn’t. This Movado Acvatic reference 18141, dating to the 1950s, deserves your full attention. At first, it reads like a classic mid-century dress watch: a well-proportioned 35mm stainless steel case, sharp lugs, 'dauphine' hands, and applied 'dagger' indices set against a warm, aged dial. But look closer, and the details begin to unfold. The dial has developed a remarkable, organic patina — a constellation of speckled aging that transforms the surface into something entirely unique. No two examples will ever age quite like this, and here, it adds depth and character in all the right ways. Then there’s the case. Signed “Acvatic”, Movado’s period spelling for “aquatic,” this model points to its then-novel waterproof construction — and more specifically, its Borgel case. Designed by François Borgel, these early waterproof cases were among the most innovative of their time, featuring a threaded design that improved water resistance well before it became industry standard. While Borgel cases are often seen in smaller, more ornate forms, finding one in a larger, round, more robust 35mm configuration like this is decidedly uncommon. A detail you might miss entirely — until you don’t. Paired with a blue leather strap, the watch balances its rugged, forward-thinking case construction with classic dress sensibility. Uncommon, quietly technical, and full of character, this is a Movado that rewards curiosity.

title: Movado 'Calatrava' Steel
description: There’s a quiet confidence to mid-century Movado that feels almost radical today. Long before the brand became synonymous with modernist minimalism, Movado was producing some of the most refined mechanical watches of the 20th century—pieces that balanced restraint, proportion, and serious watchmaking credibility. This time-only example from the 1950s–60s sits squarely in that golden era, and it remains one of the best values in vintage Swiss watchmaking. Housed in a stainless steel case, this Movado follows the same design grammar as the greatest watches of the period—think Calatrava meets field watch. Slim lugs, a domed acrylic crystal, and a balanced mid-century profile give it an elegance that feels effortless rather than showy. The dial features a lovely explorer layout in sector format. Inside beats an early Movado manual movement—robust, finely finished, and a reminder that Movado once competed directly with the best Maisons in Switzerland on a purely horological level. These movements are smooth, reliable, and far more impressive than their current market prices might suggest. Svelte, handsome, and deeply knowing, this is the kind of dress watch you buy once you’ve already “done the homework.” Understated to the point of anonymity, yet loaded with insider appeal, it’s a perfect companion for anyone who prefers their horology whispered, not shouted. A serious vintage watch for serious taste—without the serious price tag.

title: Patek Philippe Calatrava 'Diamond Hour'
description: Patek Philippe has long been known for its complicated watchmaking, but following the stock market crash of 1929, fewer clients had the means to purchase these high-end timepieces. After securing a controlling interest in the firm, Jean and Charles Henri Stern decided to to create a simple watch that would come in at a lower price point and appeal to the firm’s clientele. Using the principles espoused by the Bauhaus school and its minimalist, function-first philosophy, the company conceived the simple Reference 96. It was only in the 1980s that this series of simple dress watches would receive a dedicated collection designation, Calatrava, which takes its name from the Spanish military order established in the 12th century that serves as the symbol of Patek Philippe. These days, 'Calatrava' is a byword for a simple dress watch — even from brands other than Patek Philippe. This piece, a Reference 7200/50G-100 , takes the idea of this simple men's dress watch and adopts it into an elegant ladies' design. Housed in a sumptuous 35mm 18K white case with a signed crown and 'officer'-style lugs, it features a mother-of-pearl dial with diamond indices, a matching 'dot' outer minute track, and a matching white gold 'spade' handset. Powered by the Patek Philippe Calibre 240 ultra-thin automatic movement — whose attractive finishing and solid-gold winding rotor are visible via a sapphire caseback — this beautiful piece comes paired to a signed, blue alligator strap with square scales, tone-on-tone stitching, and a white gold deployant buckle. As a bonus, it also contains all its factory goodies and Certificate of Origin. Simply put, this excellent Calatrava is everything you need — and nothing you don’t!

title: Breitling Avenger B01  Chronograph 42
description: Breitling has long been purveyor of chronographs to pilots, and the original Breitling Chronomat models of 1942 were built from the ground up to be no-nonsense instruments for use in the cockpit. Since then, Breitling has expanded on their line of aviation watches, moving into more mechanically (and visually) complex chronographs, as well as more straightforward, legible designs such as the Avenger line. This particular piece, an Avenger B01 42, features — you guessed it — a 42mm stainless steel case with a sapphire crystal, a signed crown, and a unique dive bezel. Inside, it has a military inspired green dial with applied luminous indices and a matching handset. Powered by a COSC-certified automatic Calibre B01 movement from Breitling, it comes paired to a green canvas on leather strap with the Breitling signed stainless steel foldover deployant clasp. If you’ve been on the hunt for an excellent, serious tool watch watch with some heft, you've found it. Once more, it's from one of the best brands in the game.

title: Breitling SuperOcean Heritage Chronograph 44
description: Breitling may be best known for its aviation roots, but by the late 1950s, the brand had its sights set below the surface. As dive watches surged in popularity, led by icons like the Submariner and Fifty Fathoms, Breitling answered with the SuperOcean in 1957, introducing both a time-only diver and a chronograph built for underwater use. It marked the beginning of a nautical lineage that remains central to the brand today. This SuperOcean Heritage Chronograph 44 carries that legacy forward with a modern standpoint. Housed in a 44mm stainless steel case, the watch balances presence with purpose. The unidirectional timing bezel and screw-down architecture reinforce its dive-ready credentials, while the sapphire crystal and signed crown speak to everyday durability. The dark sunburst green dial adds depth and personality — shifting tones in the light — while applied indices with outer lume plots and Breitling’s signature hands ensure clarity in any condition. A day-and-date display adds practical functionality without overwhelming the design. Inside beats the Breitling Caliber 13, a COSC-certified automatic chronograph movement known for its reliability and ease of service. It’s paired here with a black rubber strap, giving the watch a more contemporary, sport-driven feel on the wrist. Offered as a complete set with its box, warranty card, and hangtag, this example checks all the right boxes for a modern tool watch with heritage appeal.

title: Breitling Avenger B01 Chronograph 44
description: Breitling has long been purveyor of chronographs to pilots, and the original Breitling Chronomat models of 1942 were built from the ground up to be no-nonsense instruments for use in the cockpit. Since then, Breitling has expanded on their line of aviation watches, moving into more mechanically (and visually) complex chronographs, as well as more straightforward, legible designs such as the Avenger line. This particular piece, an Avenger B01 44, features — you guessed it — a 44mm ceramic case with a sapphire crystal, a signed crown, and a unique dive bezel. Inside, it has a beautiful textured black dial with applied luminous indices and a matching handset. Powered by a COSC-certified automatic Calibre B01 movement from Breitling, it comes paired to a black canvas on leather strap with a titanium deployant clasp. If you’ve been on the hunt for an excellent, serious tool watch watch with some heft, you've found it. Once more, it's from one of the best brands in the game.

title: Breitling Top Time B01 Chronograph 'Chevrolet Corvette'
description: When Breitling launched the Top Time in the early 1960s, it was meant to be a simpler, more affordable alternative to the brand's higher-end pilot's watch offerings. Aimed at consumers 25 and other, it featured clean lines, an oversized 38mm case in steel or gold, and dials in a kaleidoscopic range of colors, including funky “radio” patterns ideal for the fashion-forward young man (or woman). The modern Top Time is no different. With its uncluttered dial designs and workhorse movement, it's an ideal entry into the Breitling catalog, which features such famous models as the Chronomat and the Navitimer. For those looking for a simpler chronograph devoid of the Navitimer's multiple timing scales, the Top Time is just the ticket. This particular Top Time, a Reference AB0176, celebrates the famous Chevrolet Corvette. Housed in a 41mm stainless steel case with a sapphire crystal, dual 'pump' pushers, and a signed crown, it features a handsome red 'sunray' dial with the Corvette logo situated beneath the Breitling wordmark; an outer multi-color tachymeter scale; a triple-register chronograph with black 'squircle' registers; and luminous 'dash' indices. With its bright orange and yellow accents against a red and black background, it's a beautiful study in chronograph design, and a cool tribute to one of America's most famous muscle cars. Powered by Breitling's B01 automatic chronograph movement and fitted to a black calfskin leather strap with a steel folding deployant buckle, the Breitling Top Time Chevrolet Corvette is the ideal driving watch — though it should do just fine outside a powerful vehicle, as well!

title: Cartier Tank ‘Onyx and Coral’
description: There are certain designs that just resonate throughout the years and remain evergreen despite changing trends and tastes. The Cartier Tank, in all its one hundred-plus years of production, is one such design. Whether in gold (yellow, pink, or white), stainless steel, or vermeil; whether Française, Chinoise, Americaine, or Normale; the Tank by Cartier is one watch that truly deserves to be called ‘iconic.’ Cartier introduced the Tank in 1917, with a run of six pieces — given, or so the legend goes, to American General Joseph Pershing and his staff. The design of the Tank was supposedly inspired by the Renault FT-17 tanks Cartier glimpsed on the battlefield of the Great War. (Cartier took the look of the Renault tank's treads and applied it to the lugs, which were integrated via brancards into the case itself.) That first run of six pieces increased to 33 by 1920, and by the end of the 20th century, that number stood well in the thousands. In sheer volume alone, the Tank — in all its various models — is Cartier's largest line of watches. This particular Tank is one of the more decorated rare iterations that few have ever seen. A Reference 78227, it measures 21 x 28mm in yellow gold, with a brilliant onyx outer strip and a coral dial center. With diamond set brancards and a diamond crown, this beautiful piece is powered by a manually-wound movement, and brings the functional form of the Tank Louis to the nines. A beautiful mix of materials and winning design language, this is a stunning rare Paris Tank for the ultimate vintage Cartier lover! What better way to break into the Cartier world than with this showstopper of a Tank?

title: Patek Philippe Ellipse
description: In 1968, Patek Philippe released the Golden Ellipse. Debuting eight years before the iconic Nautilus, the Ellipse — with its distinctive 'oval' shape — was unlike anything else in Patek Philippe’s catalogs. In period, the Ellipse line became emblematic of the era for design and signaled an elevated level of taste, particularly given the popularity of 'function-first' tool watches that defined the 1960s for many. In the intervening years, the Ellipse and many of its contemporaries fell out of favor with collectors who favored purpose built steel chronographs and divers that were closely associated with tales of adventure and exploration. Many of these wonderful, design-forward timepieces were lost in the recesses of vaults or sock drawers — or worse — melted down for their gold value. Only recently have we seen the start of an evolution in the mindset of contemporary collectors - a groundswell of appreciation for more artistic expressions of timekeeping is occurring at this very moment, and forgotten gems of the 70s and 80s are once again being discovered and appreciated for the works of horological art they truly are. This particular example, a Reference 3738-122 , dates back to the 1990s and features a 31mm x 36mm solid 18K yellow gold elliptical case with a fantastic, integrated 18K yellow gold 'rice-bead' style bracelet with a signed locking clasp, a charming blue ’sigma’ dial with applied ‘baton’ indices and a matching ‘pencil’ handset, and Patek's Calibre 240 self-winding movement. Absolutely fantastic . Watches such as this one are delightful timepieces that stand out brilliantly in a room full of steel sports watches, once again signaling an elevated understanding of what watchmaking can — and perhaps should — be. *Due to the design of this bracelet, sizing alterations may require custom work. Lead time after measurements are established is 4-6 weeks.*

title: Eska French Chronograph
description: Vintage chronographs are the soul of Analog:Shift. The perfect balance of complication, sophistication, detail in design, and staying power, vintage chronos bridge both sport and dress aesthetic in a brilliant way. As our bread and butter, we're always looking for notable and exciting chronographs for your consideration. What we have here is a great example of this. An Eska Chronograph from the 1950s, this brilliant 35mm piece was years ahead of its time and equally comfortable in the 2020s. Founded in the 1910s in Switzerland, Eska made a name for itself producing some of the most beautiful dress pieces for the market. This chronograph is fashioned from 18K gold and packs a classic manually wound movement within. With twin 'tach' and 'telemeter' scales outbound, the beautiful piece is completed with gold hands and applied numerals, 'grooved' sub-registers, and sharp faceted lugs. Not only is this piece a beautiful testament to 1950s watch design, it's in simply pristine condition throughout. For something dating to this period, it's truly hard to find something in this condition. Lesser known than its natural competition but every bit as capable, this is the type of chronograph that fits in any collection. Snag it before someone else does!

title: Jaeger-LeCoultre Reverso Day Date
description: The Reverso is, at its core, one of the earliest true sports watches—though its elegance often disguises that fact. Its sleek Art Deco lines have become inseparable from horological design history, yet the model was born not from the ballroom, but from the polo field. In the early 1930s, during a trip to India, Swiss businessman César de Trey met a British polo player who had just shattered the crystal of his watch mid-match. That conversation planted a seed: could a wristwatch be built to shield its dial from the inevitable knocks of the game? Back in Switzerland, de Trey brought the idea to Jacques-David LeCoultre, and together they refined it into a reversible case. Designed by Alfred Chauvot—who patented the concept in March 1931—the mechanism allowed the watch to flip over, protecting the crystal and dial during play. While most examples were made of steel, Jaeger-LeCoultre also produced precious-metal versions and embellished the casebacks with engraving or miniature artworks. The Reverso seen here, Reference 270.8.36 , is one of the most unique models featuring a brilliant 'day date' display. Its 26mm X 36mm stainless case holds a pointer date 'Arabic' numeral dial with 'subsidiary' seconds, a date window at 11, and a blued sword handset. This early-2000s execution is a particularly distinctive and wearable interpretation of the Reverso legacy with a unique personality unlike most on the market. A beautifully conceived piece of history—ready for the next chapter.

title: Vacheron Constantin 'Guilloche' Dress Watch
description: Simple doesn’t have to mean boring—and this watch by Vacheron Constantin proves it. As a member of the Holy Trinity of watch brands, Vacheron Constantin is generally less understood than either of its respected compatriots. VC has a legacy of producing some of the very finest high horology complications in history - and some of the loveliest. This elegant dress watch dates to circa the 1940s and marries elements that truly distinguished the manufacture in midcentury. A brilliant manually-wound beauty, this 32mm 'petite' dress watch is packed with heritage details the brand has incorporated into its vintage-inspired offerings. Incredible lugs that are a key part of the design rather than an afterthought complement a beautiful guilloche dial center and outer 'silver' timekeeping ring with applique 'dot' minute markers and gold 'explorer' and 'diamond-faceted' indexes. To top it all off, the watch is completed with a blued steel central seconds hand that adds just a little splash of color where it is needed most. Simple dress watches are one of those styles that just truly never is out of place. Dating to the golden age of dress watch design and from one of the definitive masters of dress watch execution, there's truly so much to love about this Vacheron. Plus, it's in truly excellent condition! With its timeless yet quirky looks - combined with extensive pedigree, this is a great opportunity to snag an excellent quality timepiece from one of the very best in the business!

title: Longines Weems
description: In this age of global travel, it’s easy to take for granted the sight of contrails snaking across the sky. And yet in the early years of the last century, airplanes were a new invention. But thanks to the contributions of aviation pioneers like Alberto Santos-Dumont, whose achievements in aviation are matched by those in watches, flying became more commonplace. Since aeronautical navigation posed its own particular challenges, there arose a need for specialized tools and timekeepers. Due to the distance traveled at higher speeds, any error in navigation—even if it was as little as 30 seconds off—would put the pilot off course by as much as seven miles. Therefore, just as John Harrison did for sailors in the Age of Sail with his marine chronometer, so too did Captain Philip Van Horn Weems do for pilots. A native of land-locked Tennessee, Weems must have felt the call of the sea from an early age. At just nineteen, he enrolled at the US Naval Academy in Annapolis. There he developed an aptitude for celestial navigation, and during his tenure with the Aircraft Squadron Battle Fleet, he encountered airplanes for the the first time. Despite the advances undertaken in the century between the development of John Harrison’s marine chronometer and the time Captain Weems joined the Navy, the tools and charts used in navigation were just as primitive. For example, Weems—and many of his fellow navigators—had been trained to use the American Nautical Almanac. This publication used tables, first published in 1757, that called for navigators to take measurements using observations that were so complicated that few navigators could even do them. First introduced in 1929, the Longines Weems Second-Setting Watch was the preferred timekeeping tool for navigators and pilots until the introduction of Charles Lindbergh’s version, the Hour Angle, in 1931. The Second-Setting watch allows pilots to synchronize their watches with radio signals without stopping the movement. The brilliant innovation was instrumental in the history of aviator watches. This particular iteration is one of the most valuable, executed in 14K yellow gold. Dating to the 1940s, the beautiful 28mm piece is powered by Longines manual-winding Calibre 10L and ready for a history lover's collection!

title: Patek Philippe Nautilus Chronograph
description: The Patek Philippe Nautilus is without question one of the great horological icons of the 1970s, and has in recent years once again become one of the most in-demand wristwatch models on the planet. Collectors and prospectors alike have clamored to get their hands on Genta's second great act in luxury sports watch design, and since the legendary stainless steel, time-and-date, Reference 5711 rode off into the sunset in 2021, demand has never been higher. Interestingly enough, most of the demand for Nautilus models are centered around non-complicated models, something we can't fully wrap our heads around when considering the capabilities and expertise in high horology from Patek Philippe. This example, a Reference 5980/1A-001 , resides in the complications camp, boasting a flyback chronograph complication with a mono-counter display. Wrapped in a much-adored stainless steel case as the “A” suggests in its reference designation, this Nautilus features a three-part stainless case spanning 40.5mm in diameter. Displaying a masterful blend of satin and polish finishings throughout, this piece offers a signed screw-down crown, angular chronograph pushers that parallel the case flank, articulating end-links, sapphire crystal and of course, the model’s signature ‘eared’ silhouette. Its dial is finished in a beautiful blue fumé with hallmark horizontal texturing - affectionately referred to as the 'Garage Door' by some collectors. The dial features applied luminous indices, a matching luminous handset, a date window at 3 o’clock and a mono-counter 'bullseye' chronograph register at 6 o’clock, equipped with stacked minute and hour counter hands. Its Calibre CH 28-520 C flyback chronograph movement utilizes a column wheel mechanism and vertical clutch system for dead-on accuracy rating attested by the manufacture , as well as by our measure. This Nautilus makes for a pretty epic daily driver capable of just about anything you can throw at it! Fitted to an integrated stainless steel bracelet which cohesively follows the case taper and culminates with a signed locking clasp, this comes accompanied by its complete factory kit, including its inner and outer box set, leather folio, booklets, adjustor tool, and Certificate of Origin dated April 2007. A stainless steel complicated Nautilus — does it get any better than that?

title: Audemars Piguet Ellipse
description: This slim and elegant piece couldn't be any further removed from the bulky brute of the Royal Oak Offshore line that the brand is perhaps most commonly known for amongst today's collectors. Hearkening back to a simpler time, it’s representative of an era in which the maison made so few timepieces per year that many were unique pieces, or part of incredibly limited series. This particular watch features a beautiful yellow gold case with a crisp hallmark on the 9 o'clock side, houses a lovely deep navy blue 'sunburst' dial holding a lovely 'golden flaked' patina with printed 'Arabic' indices, a matching 'pencil' handset, and an automatic winding movement within! Following a classic ellipse format, this rare timepiece comes with a date window at 3 o'clock. With its timeless and endlessly appealing looks - combined with extensive pedigree, this is a great opportunity to snag an excellent heirloom quality timepiece from one of the very best in the business! Watches such as this one are fun and wonderful timepieces, and there's no telling if and when one of these in any condition will surface again, so don't wait!

title: Omega Seamaster Planet Ocean 600M
description: The Seamaster 300, introduced in 1957, was Omega's response to the burgeoning popularity of SCUBA diving. Featuring robust steel cases with a 300-meter depth rating, rotating bezels graduated with dive-timing scales, and a metal bracelet with diver's expansion links, the early Seamaster 300s were not only functional divers that would go on to see use in Her Majesty's Navy, but are arguably some of the handsomest sports watch designs of the past century. As it evolved, the Omega Seamaster 300 went on to see numerous evolutionary changes — in fact, nearly innumerable variants in a wide variety of materials and colorways have been produced in the past 60 years. This fact alone has made the Seamaster Professional line incredibly desirable — simply because they don't all look exactly the same . One of the latest additions to the broader Seamaster collection is the Planet Ocean. Introduced in 2005, these watches features a chunkier case, a fresh bracelet design, revised design language on the dial with a 'broad arrow' handset, and increased water resistance — no doubt to compete with that of the Rolex Sea-Dweller. This particular piece, a reference 2900.50.91, is a first-gen Planet Ocean dating to the 2000s that was featured in Casino Royale . Featuring a 45.5mm twisted-lug case, a matte black dial with matching 'broad arrow' handset, a signed stainless steel multi-link bracelet with a signed steel clasp, and the always-controversial helium escape valve at 10:00, these early Planet Oceans are aging quite well and truly hit the sweet spot with respect to design, proportions, and wearability. Although large at the time, it's surprisingly versatile for daily wear. After all: If it was good enough for a certain Agent 007 on the silver screen back in 2006, then it's certainly good enough for us!

title: Patek Philippe Dress Watch
description: Though the famous Calatrava is — for good reason — perhaps the best known of Patek Philippe’s incredible dress watches, there are myriad others that deserve recognition. From the rectangular Gondolo to the curvaceous Golden Ellipse, these timepieces, though simple in design, beg a closer look for their classic proportions and strict attention to detail. Patek has been producing such wristwatches for roughly a century, and shows no signs of slowing down. A Reference 3729J, this curious late 1970s/early 1980s dress watch is a prime example of the maison's adventurous spirit. Measuring 28mm x 34mm, it consists of an elongated, octagonal yellow gold case with a stepped bezel, a small winding crown with an onyx cabochon, and an acrylic crystal. The dial is subtle but striking: Rather than lacquer, it's actually a thin slice of onyx overlaid with...well, nothing! Only the Patek Philippe signature and a set of yellow gold 'dauphine' hands disturb its quiet elegance. Powered by the hand-wound Calibre 215 and paired to a black alligator leather strap with a yellow gold-tone pin buckle, this incredible dress watch is an understated reminder of the sheer breadth of the Patek Philippe catalogue. Who wouldn't want to proudly wear such a creative piece of haute horlogerie ?

title: Breitling Top Time B01 Chronograph Ford Mustang
description: When Breitling launched the Top Time in the early 1960s, it was meant to be a simpler, more affordable alternative to the brand's higher-end pilot's watch offerings. Aimed at consumers 25 and other, it featured clean lines, an oversized 38mm case in steel or gold, and dials in a kaleidoscopic range of colors, including funky “radio” patterns ideal for the fashion-forward young man (or woman). The modern Top Time is no different. With its uncluttered dial designs and workhorse movement, it's an ideal entry into the Breitling catalog, which features such famous models as the Chronomat and the Navitimer. For those looking for a simpler chronograph devoid of the Navitimer's multiple timing scales, the Top Time is just the ticket. This particular Top Time, a Reference AB01762A1L1A1, celebrates the famous Ford Mustang. Housed in a 41mm stainless steel case with a sapphire crystal, dual pump pushers, and a signed crown, it features a handsome green sunray dial with the Mustang logo situated beneath the Breitling wordmark; an outer multi-color tachymeter scale; a triple-register chronograph with black "squircle" registers; and luminous 'dash' indices. With its bright orange and yellow accents against a green and black background, it's a beautiful study in chronograph design, and a cool tribute to one of America's most famous muscle cars. Powered by Breitling's B01 automatic chronograph movement and fitted to a heritage style mesh bracelet, the Breitling Top Time Ford Mustang is the ideal driving watch — though it should do just fine outside a powerful vehicle, as well!

title: Universal Genève Unisonic-Sub
description: By now, Universal Genève should be well known in the watch collecting community. The brand attained a reputation in its heyday for well-constructed, aesthetically-appealing designs, typified in the Compax and Polerouter models. Yet even with its commitment to aesthetic excellence, Universal Genève was no stranger to innovation. Bulova's introduction of the Accutron 218 movement in 1965 intrigued and impressed brands and consumers alike, and Universal Genève sought to implement this new technology a decade before the Quartz Revolution changed the watchmaking game forever. The Unisonic was the result — a stunning and innovative timepiece that merged UG's attentive Swiss design language with a futuristic American movement, a marriage artfully denoted by the unique UG/Accutron logo printed on the watch's dial. (Cool, right?) If that weren't enough, this particular Unisonic is a Sub — a diver model that took the Unisonic design a step further...or, shall we say, a fathom deeper ? Housed in a chunky, brushed 43mm stainless steel cushion case with a signed crown, an elapsed time bezel with a blue, fully graduated insert, and a unique 14-sided steel inner bezel that protrudes upward surrounding the watch's acrylic crystal, it features a colorful dial with an outer minute track in white with black printing, an inner black dial with applied, luminous blue indices with tritium material, a framed date window at 3 o'clock, and a special handset with a stubby white hour hand, an orange 'sword' minute hand, and a white central seconds hand. As if that weren't enough, the Unisonic Sub comes paired to a signed, integrated multi-link bracelet from Gay Frères punched with a '3 69' date code. You don't see that every day, folks! Coupling the elegance of Universal Genève with true technological innovation, this is a piece of horological history, a relic from that transitional period between the introduction of the tuning fork and the Quartz Revolution. If you love tool watches, horological history, and technological innovation, then this one is for you!

title: Bulova Accutron 'Dynamic'
description: Before quartz conquered the world, there was a hum. The 1970s Bulova Accutron Dynamic captures that strange, optimistic moment when the future sounded different—literally. Powered by Bulova’s revolutionary tuning fork movement, the Accutron didn’t tick. It vibrated at 360 hertz, producing a faint, electric hum that felt closer to laboratory equipment than traditional watchmaking. Space-age accuracy, Cold War confidence. Joseph Bulova founded his company in 1875, later establishing a modern movement-manufacturing facility in Biel, Switzerland, in 1912, and applying American mass-production techniques to watchmaking. By mid-century, Bulova had become one of the most influential horological manufacturers in the United States—known as much for its technical ambition as for its forward-thinking design and advertising. This 38mm steel example leans fully into the era’s design language. The case is broad and clean, crown tucked at 4 o’clock and signed with the slick Accutron logo—a subtle but intentional shift from convention. The sector black dial is stark and graphic: silver baton markers, bold red accents at 3, 6, 9, and 12, white 'baton' hands, and a vivid red central seconds hand slicing across the surface with smooth, uninterrupted motion. No tick. Just glide. Under frost-free plexi, the dial has that slightly softened warmth only acrylic can deliver. It’s industrial, but not cold. Technical, but playful. The Accutron Dynamic isn’t just a watch—it’s a reminder of when American innovation genuinely rattled Swiss nerves. A reminder of the moment when the future arrived on the wrist, this Accutron remains as compelling today as it was in its humming, space-age prime.

title: Cartier Tank Americaine Automatic
description: The Cartier Tank Americaine is quite possibly the least understood model in Cartier's lineup. Introduced in 1989, it is, in many ways, a modern update to the classic Cintrée model. With an elongated case featuring a gently curved caseback, the collection was designed from the start to be a larger watch to suit American tastes. Initially launched exclusively with quartz movements, it has expanded in the past decades to include a variety of mechanical variants as well as sizes, case materials, and complications that put it all over the map in terms of value. This particular example is a stunning full-size Reference 1740 cased in 18K yellow gold, and features the timeless satin silver guilloché dial with printed 'Roman' indices upon which Cartier was built. Fitted with a set of blued steel 'sword' hands, an octagonal sapphire cabochon winding crown, an automatic movement, and an absolutely magnificent matching yellow gold bracelet with deployant clasp, it's quite simply one of the most gorgeous (and heavy!) Tanks we've ever had the pleasure of offering for sale. Accompanied by its correct inner Cartier box, this Tank Americaine is the type of spectacular dress watch that will simply never go out of style. If you're jonesing for a hunk of yellow gold on your wrist — that happens to tell the time! — you simply can't go wrong with a Reference 1740.

title: Chopard Grand Prix De Monaco Historique Chronograph
description: The Grand Prix de Monaco Historique is a biannual event that brings together drivers and enthusiasts of historical cars to witness what vintage race cars were born to do –race. Taking place at the very same circuit as the Formula 1 Monaco Grand Prix since 1997, the Grand Prix de Monaco Historique is a celebration of speed, sound, and beauty. With a deep connection to motorsport that dates back to the Mille Miglia, it was only natural that Chopard was chosen as a sponsor and official timekeeper for the growing event. To commemorate the Grand Prix de Monaco Historique, Chopard unveiled several models made to go hard and fast. Or as we say, never lift . This particular Chopard Grand Prix de Monaco Historique Chronograph ref. 168992-3032 is part of the brand’s Classic Racing collection and was released to celebrate Chopard’s longstanding partnership with the famed historic motorsport event in Monaco. Housed in a lightweight 42mm titanium case, the watch pairs sporting performance with subtle motorsport cues. A grey PVD tachymeter ring frames the matte grey dial, which features a circular-grained finish, luminous stick markers, and bright orange accents on the seconds hand, giving the piece an unmistakably racing-inspired look. The chronograph layout features three subdials for running seconds, a 30-minute timer, and a 12-hour counter, along with a date display at 3 o’clock. Powering the watch is a COSC-certified automatic chronometer movement offering approximately 46 hours of power reserve, underscoring Chopard’s commitment to precision. Completing the theme is a perforated black calfskin strap with orange stitching, a deliberate nod to vintage driving gloves and classic racing culture. The sapphire crystal exhibition case back also features the Automobile Club de Monaco logo. There’s no question that cars and watches go together. And when they come together in watch form, man, do we love it, and we’re sure you will too.

title: Longines 'Salmon' Pointer Date
description: In our opinion, Longines doesn't quite get the respect that they deserve. That isn’t to say that they aren't held in high regard. But the level of innovation, craftsmanship, and design that Longines has demonstrated over the course of their history simply cannot be understated, and certainly extends to a wide variety of timepieces across the board. Take this vintage dress watch, for example: Dating to the 1940s and housed in a 36mm stainless steel case with an acrylic crystal and a brushed bezel, it features one of the most beautiful salmon dials in stellar condition with a pointer date -- a distinctive signature for Longines. The stainless steel Calatrava style case is also in great condition with a concave bezel. For a 1940s piece, this watch was far ahead of its time, and its midcentury pieces like this one that are the inspiration for many of the modern watches the brand makes. This is the type of versatile piece that can be worn everyday, or slipped onto an alligator leather strap for dressier occasions. Perfect for both men and women, it features a stunning dial configuration that simply isn’t found on many modern timepieces, and we love it. This piece reminds us why Longines pieces from this period are becoming increasingly coveted in serious collecting circles.

title: Patek Philippe 'Tank'
description: Though the famous Calatrava is — for good reason — perhaps the best known of Patek Philippe’s incredible dress watches, there are myriad others that deserve recognition. From the rectangular Gondolo to the curvaceous Golden Ellipse, these timepieces, though simple in design, beg a closer look for their classic proportions and strict attention to detail. Patek has been producing such wristwatches for roughly a century, and shows no signs of slowing down. This particular Patek Philippe dress watch is perfect for someone who loves the Tank (and Tank-like) dress watch: A Reference 3405J from the 1960s, it features a beautifully sized, square 27mm x 27mm case with downturned lugs, a smooth bezel, a signed crown, and an acrylic crystal atop a rectangular silvered dial with thin, applied gold 'stick' indices and a matching 'pencil' handset. Powered by the hand-wound Patek Philippe Calibre 23-300 movement and paired to a brown Louisiana alligator leather strap with a signed yellow gold pin buckle, this gorgeous piece is a vestige of a classier time. Strap it on and be instantly transported to midcentury New York City for a bit of Mad Men- esque glamor!

title: Girard Perregaux Chronograph 'Ecurie Francorchamps'
description: There’s no question that cars and watches go together. But in the realm of automotive-inspired timepieces, brands often over-do the automotive theme to the point where the entire thing looks, well, gimmicky. There are only a handful of executions that tastefully blend both worlds. Less is more, after all. As is such with the Girard-Perregaux Chronograph 'Ecurie Francorchamps.' A limited edition from the early 2000s, it celebrates the 50th anniversary of Écurie Francorchamps , the Belgian racing team founded by Jacques Swaters that competed multiple times in the 24 Hours of Le Mans. Housed in a 38mm stainless steel case with pump pushers, a fluted signed crown, a stepped bezel, and an engraved commemorative caseback, it features a decidedly 1940s-influenced white dial with an outer tachymeter scale, applied 'Arabic' indices, a triple-register chronograph, a 4:30 date window, and a silver ' feuille ' handset. Powered by the Girard-Perregaux Calibre 3370 movement — an ETA base movement with a Dubois-Dépraz chronograph module — it comes paired to a brown leather strap with tone-on-tone stop stitching and a signed stainless steel pin buckle. Limited to 100 pieces, it also features its inner and outer box, pamphlets, and an extra strap. If this handsome piece doesn't make you want to fire up your vintage Jaguar C-type — we don't know what will!

title: Universal Genève Cioccolatone 'J.W. Benson'
description: While Universal Genève may be most famed for its Compax chronographs and the aeronautically-inspired Polerouter, its svelte dress watches are equally compelling. Throughout the latter half of the 20th century, UG designed simple, time-only watches alongside its more complicated brethren that played with shape, color, and design in interesting and novel ways. This particular dress watch is referred to as a 'Cioccolatone' ('big chocolate') after its distinctive, chocolate candy-shaped case that was used by numerous marques beginning in the 1950s. Measuring 25.5mm in yellow gold with beautifully flowing 'scroll' lugs, an unsigned crown, and an acrylic crystal, it features a handsome silver guilloché dial in a C lous de Paris pattern with applied gold faceted indices, a matching gold 'dauphine' handset, and a double-signature: In addition to the Universal Genève wordmark, it also bears the name of J.W. Benson, a well-respected London clock and watchmaker turned retailer, now defunct. Powered by a hand-wound U.G. movement and paired to a lovely brown alligator strap with tone-on-tone stitching and a signed gold-tone steel U.G. pin buckle, this watch is the perfect entryway into Universal Genève for the budding collector. Sure to garner knowing nods of approval from the inner enthusiast circle, it's quite simply a killer piece from one of our absolute favorite brands — full stop.

title: Bulova 'Fancy Lug' Dress Watch
description: Mid-century Bulova had a knack for making everyday watches feel architectural. During the 1940s and ’50s, the American brand produced a wide range of thoughtfully designed dress pieces — watches that balanced affordability with surprising creativity in case shapes, dial textures, and applied markers. This 1959 L9 (L=1950s, 9 = '59) Bulova dress watch is a perfect example of that spirit. Housed in a 14K yellow gold case measuring 33mm, the watch immediately stands out thanks to its sharply sculpted angular lugs, giving the otherwise classic round case a bold, almost modernist profile. There’s a hint of WPA-era Depression Modern design in the geometry — clean lines, purposeful shapes, and just enough flourish to keep things interesting. The dial is equally compelling. Rendered in a soft cream tone with a textured inner ring, it features large, stylized applied 'dart' markers that catch the light beautifully. Slender 'alpha' hands sweep across the dial, while a sub-seconds register at six o’clock adds both visual balance and mechanical charm. At just 6.3mm thick, the watch wears slim and elegant on the wrist, powered by a 17-jewel manual-winding 11AF movement that reflects Bulova’s long tradition of reliable watchmaking. Paired with a brown leather strap and gold-tone pin buckle, this piece captures the understated sophistication of late-1950s American design. Angular, refined, and quietly distinctive — proof that even the simplest dress watch can have real personality.

title: Patek Philippe Calatrava
description: First presented in 1932 with the Reference 96, the Patek Philippe Calatrava is the benchmark for classic men’s dress watches. The groundbreaking reference set the stage for nearly every piece Patek has released since, and became the blueprint for other brand’s approaches to classy men’s timepieces. Heavily influenced by the Bauhaus design philosophy, the Calatrava was then considered extremely modern with its minimalist, less-is-more approach to watch design. The Calatrava 96 was also one of the first pieces the brand released after the Stern family purchased a majority share in Patek Philippe in the early '30s. In many respects, the 96 is emblematic of the Stern family’s contributions to Patek. The 96 was one of the earliest round wristwatches Patek ever made, and was also the very first serially-produced piece to receive its own reference. In the years since 1932, Patek has released dozens of Calatrava references, each with its own idiosyncrasies. Almost all share the underlying DNA of the 96, prioritizing subtle elegance above all else. This piece, a Reference 5227G-010, is the very image of the elegant Calatrava design but in a modern silhouette. With a highly comfortable 39mm 18K white gold case and measuring just 9mm thick, it’s an incredibly easy-wearing watch. The case features downturned lugs, a polished bezel, a sapphire crystal, a signed crown and an officer-style caseback that, when opened, reveals the maison’s Calibre 324 SC self-winding movement. Furnished with applied white gold indices, a ‘dot’ minute track, a matching 'dauphine' handset, and a framed date window at 3 o’clock set against an black tone, the dial is an exercise of simplistic beauty. Outfitted to a signed black alligator leather strap with an 18K white gold pin buckle, this piece is an exceptional example of the traditional Calatrava design and the maison’s artisanship. It also happens to come with its inner box and Certificate of Origin. Simply put, this Calatrava is everything you need in a dress watch, and nothing you don’t!

title: Jaeger-LeCoultre Reverso Classic Duetto
description: The Reverso, Jaeger-LeCoultre's most distinctive watch, certainly has an impressive pedigree. One of the world's first sports watches, the Reverso combined utility with a sleek and elegant Art Deco exterior that has become an icon of horological design. But in the case of the Reverso, looks deceive. Though the model appears at first glance an Art Deco dress watch, it owes its conception to the gentlemanly sport of polo. While touring India in the 1920s, Swiss businessman César de Trey met a British polo player who'd just cracked the crystal of his watch on the field. De Trey returned to Switzerland with an idea that had taken root in his head, which he then discussed with Jacques-David LeCoultre: What if a watch could be made with a case that could reverse? The reversible case, masterminded by designer Alfred Chauvot, was Jaeger-LeCoultre's solution to the impact problem polo players could face on the field. Once reversed, the delicate components of the dial were protected from the shocks sustained while on horseback. No more would polo players risk cracking crystals or receiving damage to the dials of their watches. Chauvot took out a patent for the Reverso in March 1931. Produced chiefly in steel, Jaeger-LeCoultre also released the Reverso in gold, and decorated the case back with engravings or even miniature portraits. But with changing tastes in the 1970s — for round watches as well as quartz movements — Jaeger-LeCoultre ceased production of the Reverso. The model lay dormant for years until its triumphant re-issue in 1982. While those watches contained quartz movements, their success represented a future for the Reverso — which has since returned to its previous tentpole status within the greater JLC lineup. This example, a Reference Q2668432, features a small sized stainless steel 21mm x 34mm reversible case, sapphire crystal, signed crown, and a silver tone dial with printed 'Arabic' indices with a rectangular central minutes track framing a guilloche dial center and blued-steel 'sword' handset. The reverse displays a secondary time display with diamonds at the top and bottom. Powered by a Jaeger-LeCoultre Calibre 844 manual movement, is truly one of the most classic layouts available. This example also comes complete with its factory goodies. In terms of elegant design and horological prowess, this watch punches way above its weight class — offering tremendous looks and street cred with its classic silhouette — the way only a Reverso can!

title: Cartier Tank Américaine
description: There are Cartier watches everyone understands immediately—and then there are the ones that sneak up on you. The Tank Américaine lives firmly in the latter camp. It doesn’t shout like a Santos or lean too heavily on the purity of the Tank Louis. Instead, it stretches, curves, and refines, like a familiar silhouette seen through a slightly surreal perspective. Introduced in 1989, the Américaine was Cartier’s quietly confident response to shifting tastes. Where the Cintrée of the 1920s was elegant and aristocratic, the Américaine is its more relaxed, late-20th-century descendant. The case shape is longer and softer, and it is designed with a distinctly transatlantic point of view. The gently arched case back hugs the wrist in a way that feels almost ergonomic, while the elongated proportions give it a presence that accentuates its chill. This example rendered in 18K yellow gold hits a particularly sweet spot. At 24 X 41 mm, it wears with intention but never excess. The white dial—clean, graphic, unmistakably Cartier—keeps things grounded, while the addition of an automatic movement and date complication nudges it firmly into daily-wear territory. It’s an understated Tank that doesn’t require ceremony. The broader Américaine line has long suffered from an identity crisis—quartz, mechanical, small, large, precious, accessible. But pieces like this cut through the noise. This is the model distilled: elegant, wearable, and just a touch unexpected. The Américaine is the thinking person’s Tank. A little off-center, a little misunderstood—and all the better for it.

title: Girard-Perregaux Gyromatic
description: Few names in Swiss watchmaking carry the quiet authority of Girard-Perregaux. With roots tracing back to the late 18th century, the manufacture has weathered revolutions in taste and technology alike — from early adoption of wristwatches to meaningful contributions to quartz engineering. But long before high-frequency oscillators and Laureato hype, GP excelled at something far more elemental: the perfectly judged dress watch. This automatic winding dress watch is an excellent example of what we mean. In the 1950s, the newest developments in watch technology revolved around automatic winding movement development, and GP was at the forefront. In 1950, GP presented the Gyromatic, which replaced ratchet winding with roller clutches that are far more efficient. The Gyromatic allowed Girard-Perregaux to develop slimmer, more efficient compact dress watches. This piece is just one example. Fashioned from stainless steel, this dress swatch features stronger 3,6,9, and 12 indices, with further applied indices for the minor indexes. The dial is fashioned in a silver hue and is topped off with a 'pencil' handset. Slim, elegant, and refreshingly unfussy, this is the kind of watch that makes a strong case for restraint. It also marks an important part in the evolution of watches in general with advanced automatic winding movement systems.

title: Cartier Santos Carrée Two-Tone
description: The story of the Cartier Santos begins in the early 1900s with the pioneering aviator Alberto Santos-Dumont. Born in 1873, Santos-Dumont developed an early fascination with engineering and flight, eventually becoming one of aviation’s most daring and celebrated figures. His experiments with balloons, airships, and primitive aircraft captured the imagination of Paris — and brought him into the orbit of Louis Cartier. Frustrated with the difficulty of checking a pocket watch while piloting, Santos-Dumont mentioned the problem to his friend. Cartier’s solution, introduced in 1904, was radical for its time: a purpose-built wristwatch designed for flight. With its square case, visible screws, and sturdy construction, the Santos became one of the first modern wristwatches, and arguably the world’s first sports watch. First produced commercially in 1911, the Santos has remained one of Cartier’s most enduring designs. Over the decades, the maison has reinterpreted the model in countless forms, but the fundamental design language has remained remarkably consistent. This particular example is the Santos Carrée, a reference that emerged during the late 1970s as Cartier reintroduced the Santos for a new generation. With its sharp-edged case and integrated bracelet, the Carrée embraced the bold, geometric style of the era while staying true to the original Santos DNA. Measuring 30mm by 41mm, this two-tone example pairs stainless steel with 18K yellow gold across the case and bracelet, creating a look that is unmistakably Cartier. The white dial features the brand’s signature Roman numerals, a chemin de fer (railway) minute track, blued steel sword hands, and a date aperture at 3 o’clock. Powered by an automatic movement and paired with its matching two-tone bracelet, the watch is accompanied by a satin-lined presentation box. Iconic, versatile, and effortlessly recognizable, the Santos Carrée remains one of Cartier’s most enduring designs. Proof that true icons never really go out of style.

title: Certina DS-2
description: When it comes to vintage dive watches, we love the heavy hitters just as much as the next guy. The Rolex Submariner, the Blancpain Fifty Fathoms, and the Omega Seamaster 300 all have special places in our hearts - and collections. But for every well-known name in the dive watch world, there’s one or two that make us stop and say, “Now this is why we got into collecting watches in the first place.” In the 1950s and 1960s, lots of brands got into the dive watch game, some using contract cases and movements, while others went down the route of building a purpose built, ground-up, sea going weapon to vanquish its competition. The Certina DS-2 falls into that select group of watches. This brilliantly over-engineered 200 meter diver was made in the 1970s and features a lovely blue dial. Featuring a 40mm stainless steel cushion case housing an automatic Calibre 25-651 movement, its screwdown caseback and rotating bezel assembly are instantly identifiable to dive watch fanatics the world over with a profile that is not easily forgotten. This particular example remains in great condition throughout with a brilliantly patinated dial, timing bezel insert, unsigned screw-down crown, caseback with Certina engravings in deep relief. Its not often that a funky, colorful, 70s diving watch becomes an artifact sought after by dive watch enthusiasts - but the Certina DS-2 is a rare bird that checks all of the boxes!

title: Cartier Tank LC Diamonds
description: There are certain designs that resonate throughout the years and remain evergreen despite changing trends and tastes. The Cartier Tank, in all its one hundred-plus years of production, is one such design. Whether in gold (yellow, pink, or white), stainless steel, or vermeil; whether Française, Chinoise, Americaine, or Normale; the Tank by Cartier is one watch that truly deserves to be called iconic . Cartier introduced the Tank in 1917, with a run of six pieces — given, or so the legend goes, to American General Joseph Pershing and his staff. The design of the Tank was inspired by the Renault FT-17 tanks Cartier glimpsed on the battlefield of the Great War. (Cartier took the look of the Renault's treads and applied it to the lugs, which were integrated via brancards into the case itself.) That first run of six pieces increased to 33 by 1920, and by the end of the 20th century, that number stood well in the thousands. In sheer volume alone, the Tank — in all its various models — is Cartier's largest line of watches. The Tank Louis was introduced in 1922, following close on the heels of the Asian-influenced Tank Chinoise. With the Tank Louis, Cartier went back to basics, with the classic lugs that resemble the treads that inspired them. Louis Cartier himself wore the watch that bore his name — that association with the man himself marks the Tank Louis as the quintessence of the Cartier Tanks. This particular Tank Louis, a Reference 96036 , dates to the 1980s and features a sumptuous 24mm x 31mm 18K yellow gold case with a sapphire crystal, a sapphire cabochon winding crown, a diamond-set bezel, and a spectacular gold multi-tone dial with diamond-set indices and a gold 'sword' handset. Powered by a hand-wound movement, it comes fitted to a Cartier-signed brown leather strap with a signed yellow gold deployant clasp. Beloved by movie stars, heads of state (and First Ladies), and even four-star generals, the Tank Louis Carter is a necessity for those who want to add class, style and distinction to their horological arsenal. This exceptional Tank Louis upholds the legend and is a sure to be a treasured edition to any collection.

title: Patek Philippe Neptune
description: Patek Philippe made a fantastic, stainless steel sports watch — and we're not talking about the Aquanaut or the Nautilus. The Patek Philippe Neptune has long been under-appreciated since its debut in 1996. Both it and the Aquanaut (which also came out in 1996) served as alternatives to the popular Nautilus, and were meant to inject some life and energy to the the maison's sports watch collection. The Neptune in its multiple iterations featured its own unique design language, with its round case equipped with crown guards, svelte proportions, and an excellent integrated multi-link bracelet. This piece, a Reference 5080/1A, features a 36mm stainless steel case with signature crown guards, a signed crown, a sapphire crystal, and a stunning fluted bezel with polished hour markers. Its luminous black dial, meanwhile, features applied quadrant 'Roman' indices, a date window at 3 o'clock, a luminous feuille handset, and a central chemin de fer minute track. Powered by a self-winding Patek Philippe Calibre 315 movement, this piece comes paired to a stainless steel integrated bracelet with matching finishing and a signed locking deployant clasp. Instantly recognizable to those who appreciate the quirkier corners of the Patek oeuvre , the Neptune is almost certainly a future classic. Heck — in our books, it already belongs in a [design] museum!

title: Patek Philippe Annual Calendar
description: In 1991, the soon-to-be graduates at the Geneva School of Engineering were presented with their final project prior to receiving their diploma — simplify the perpetual calendar. Although the task was easier said than done, their work led to the inception of the annual calendar complication, patented by Patek in collaboration with the bright engineering minds at the GSE. Five years later, Patek debuted the Reference 5035 which marked the world’s first-ever annual calendar movement and finally bridged the gap between a triple date function and the formidable quantième perpétuel . This piece transports us forward to the present day and is a handsome modern interpretation of the Patek Philippe Complications Annual Calendar. Designated as a Reference 5205R-001, this lovely timepiece features a 40mm 18K rose gold case with sculpted, hollowed lugs, a concave bezel, and polished finish. The white/silver, grained sector dial features a set of applied rose gold indices and presents a set of day-date-month apertures at 12 o’clock, a subsidiary pointer-date dial with a moon phase display and a ‘dauphine’ handset. T he back side features an exhibition back providing a window to admire the self-winding Caliber 324 S QA LU and its 21K gold winding rotor adorned with the hallmark Patek Philippe seal . It comes fitted to a signed alligator strap with a matching 18K rose gold pin buckle. There’s no doubt the Nautilus and Aquanaut have gotten their time in the sun, and even vintage Patek Philippe in general continues to demand the attention of any savvy collector. But let us not pass over the high-complication heavy-hitters hiding in plain sight in Patek’s modern catalog!

title: Breitling Aviator 765 AVI 1953 Re-Edition
description: Breitling was an early pioneer in the development of precision-made cockpit instruments for pilots. In the 1930s, its dashboard timers paved the way for its later Chronomat and Navitimer series of wrist-worn timepieces, many of which still feature prominently in the Breitling catalog today. The Ref. 765 AVI, released in the 1950s, was a seminal reference that further established the Swiss brand's aeronautical bona fides. This modern version, the Aviator 765 AVI 1953 Re-Edition, offers the looks of the original with remarkable faith to details — in fact, virtually the only changes are the absence of the word "Genève" from the dial and an increase in water resistance to 30m. As far as vintage reissues go, this one is among the best. Housed in a 41 stainless steel case with dual pump pushers, a signed crown, a sapphire crystal, and a rotating, bidirectional 12-hour bezel, it features a handsome, highly legible matte-black dial with an outer 1/5th-seconds track; large, luminous 'Arabic' indices; a triple-register chronograph layout with sunken, black totalizers and a luminous 'syringe' handset. Within the watch, the COSC-certified Breitling Calibre B09 hand-wound movement with 70 hours of power reserve provides exceptional timekeeping, while a black calfskin leather strap with white contrast stitching offers a comfortable wearing experience. With its knurled bezel and crisp pusher action, the Aviator 765 AVI 1953 is every bit the faithful tool watch, too. The only thing that doesn't come with this piece? A pilot's license. You'll have to take care of that one on your own!

title: Patek Philippe Calatrava
description: Patek Philippe has long been known for its complicated watchmaking, but following the stock market crash of 1929, fewer clients had the means to purchase these high-end timepieces. After securing a controlling interest in the firm, Jean and Charles Henri Stern decided to create a simple watch that would come in at a lower price point and appeal to the firm’s clientele. Using the principles espoused by the Bauhaus school and its minimalist, function-first philosophy, Patek conceived the Calatrava, taking its name from the Spanish military order established in the 12th century which had become the symbol of Patek Philippe. Since its debut in 1932, the Calatrava has become a byword for a simple, time-only dress watch — even from brands other than Patek itself. This piece, a Reference 6007G-010, isn’t your run-of-the-mill Calatrava. Debuted in 2023 in three individual colorways — red, blue and yellow — this particular Calatrava features an array of automotive-eqsque design cues that easily makes it the sportiest 'Trava in their modern lineup. Housed in a 40mm 18K white gold case, this piece features a polished bezel and case complemented by a sapphire crystal and a signed crown. The black luminous dial features a matching white gold handset and radially configured, luminous, white gold Arabic indices. Straying away from the traditional dressier aesthetics of the Calatrava model, the Patek Philippe design team elected to add sportier design notes with its center dial adorned with a 'carbon' finish and a red accented, checker-style, minute track. Outfitted to a signed black leather strap with a matching 'carbon' embossing, contrast red stitching and a spade-style 18K white gold pin buckle, this piece comes with all of its factory goodies including its inner box, out box, signed leather folio, books and Certificate of Origin dated 2023. Traditional Calatrava aesthetics were thrown out the window on this one and we're here for it!

title: Longines 1938 Chronostop
description: Collectors of vintage chronographs should know Longines well. Their chronographs of the 1930s and 1940s represent the pinnacle of chronograph production. This excellence in timekeeping is typified in the minds of collectors by the 13ZN. Released in 1936, the 13ZN was the first chronograph wristwatch to feature a flyback function, which allows the wearer to reset the chronograph without having to stop it first. Upon its release, the 13ZN became a favorite among aviators, who used the feature for navigation, and has gained something of a cult status among modern collectors.
